title: What to Prune and What Not to Prune at Initialization

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - WHAT TO PRUNE AND WHAT NOT TO PRUNE AT INI TIALIZATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Post-training dropout based approaches achieve high sparsity and are well established means of deciphering problems relating to computational cost and overfitting in Neural Network architectures citesrivastava2014dropout, (Pan et al., 2016), Zhu & Gupta (2017), LeCun et al. (1990). Contrastingly, pruning at initialization is still far behind Frankle et al. (2020). Initialization pruning is more efficacious when it comes to scaling computation cost of the network. Furthermore, it handles overfitting just as well as post training dropout. It is also averse to retraining losses.

In approbation of the above reasons, the paper presents two approaches to prune at initialization. The goal is to achieve higher sparsity while preserving performance. 1) K-starts, begins with k random p-sparse matrices at initialization. In the first couple of epochs the network then determines the ”fittest” of these p-sparse matrices in an attempt to find the ”lottery ticket” Frankle & Carbin (2018) p-sparse network. The approach is adopted from how evolutionary algorithms find the best individual. Depending on the Neural Network architecture, fitness criteria can be based on magnitude of network weights, magnitude of gradient accumulation over an epoch or a combination of both. 2) Dissipating gradients approach, aims at eliminating weights that remain within a fraction of their initial value during the first couple of epochs. Removing weights in this manner despite their magnitude best preserves performance of the network. Contrarily, the approach also takes the most epochs to achieve higher sparsity. 3) Combination of dissipating gradients and kstarts outperforms either methods and random dropout consistently.

The benefits of using the provided pertaining approaches are: 1) They do not require specific knowledge of the classification task, fixing of dropout threshold or regularization parameters 2) Retraining of the model is neither necessary nor affects the performance of the p-sparse network.
We evaluate the efficacy of the said methods on Autoencoders and Fully Connected Multilayered Perceptrons. The datasets used are MNIST and Fashion MNIST.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Computational complexity and overfitting in neural networks is a well established problem Frankle & Carbin (2018), Han et al. (2015), LeCun et al. (1990), Denil et al. (2013). We utilize pruning approaches for the following two reasons: 1) To reduce the computational cost of a fully connected neural network. 2) To reduce overfitting in the network.

Given a large number of post-training pruning approaches Srivastava et al. (2014), Geman et al. (1992), Pan et al. (2016), the paper attempts to propose two pre-training pruning approaches: kstarts and dissipating gradients. Moreover, it appears to be the case that when isolated from other factors sparse networks outperform fully connected networks. When not isolated they perform at least as well up to a percentage of sparsity depending on the number of parameters in the said network. kstarts and dissipating gradients provide are simple nevertheless effective methods to quickly look for best sparse networks to.
The approaches exploit the knowledge that a network has multiple underlying p-sparse networks that perform just as well and in some cases even better when contrasted with their fully connected

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

counterparts Frankle & Carbin (2018). What percentage of sparsity is realized, depends largely on the number of parameters originally present in the network. Such sparse networks are potent in preventing over-fitting and reducing computational cost.

The poset-training pruning has several approaches in place such as adding various regularization schemes to prune the network Louizos et al. (2017), Pan et al. (2016) or using second derivative or hessian of the weights for dropout LeCun et al. (1990), Hassibi & Stork (1993). Han et al. (2015), Alford et al. (2019), Zhu & Gupta (2017) use an efficient iterative pruning method to iteratively increase sparsity. Srivastava et al. (2014) dropout random hidden units with p probability instead of weights to avoid overfitting in general. Each of these approaches is effective and achieves good sparsity post-training.

We use a simple intuitive models that achieve good results and exploits the fact that a number of sub networks in a Neural Network has the potential to individually learn the input Srivastava et al. (2014). We decide on a sparse network early on based on the dropout method and use only that for training. This provides an edge for faster computation, quicker elimination of excess weights and reduced generalization error. The sparsity achieved is superior to random dropout.

Section II gives a general introduction to all the methods, section III defines p-sparsity, section IV provides the algorithm for both approaches, section V describes experimental setup and results, section VI discusses various design choices, section VII gives a general discussion of results, section VIII discusses limitations of the approach and section IX provides conclusions and final remarks.

## 2 PRUNING METHODS

## 2.1 KSTARTS

## 2.1.1 KSTARTS AND EVOLUTIONARY ALGORITHMS

We take the concept of k random starts from Evolutionary Algorithms (Vikhar, 2016) that use a fitness function or heuristic to perform ”natural selection” in optimization and search based problems (Goldberg & Holland, 1988). It is relatively simple to fit genetic algorithms to the problem at hand. Other method that would be equally effective with a little bit of modification are Hunting Search (Oftadeh et al., 2010), Natural Adaptation Strategies (Wierstra et al., 2008), firefly algorithm (Yang, 2010) etc.

The basic components of the algorithm are: (1) **Population** : A product of network weights and sparse matrices. (2) **Individual** : An instance of the population. (3) **Fitness Function** : The heuristic chosen for evaluation of the population.

## 2.1.2 POPULATION

We first initialize K sparse matrices, a single instance of these K sparse matrices can be seen in equation **??** . In every iteration we multiply model weights W of the Network layer in question with every instance of the K sparse matrices. The resulting set of matrices is our population for that iteration. Each iteration is referred to as a new generation.

_population_ = _W ∗ K − SparseMatrices_

(1)

## 2.1.3 INDIVIDUAL

Each individual, in a population of K instances, is a sparse matrix of size equal to the size of network weights, W. The number of 0’s and 1’s in the sparsity matrix are determined by the connectivity factor p which is further described in section 3. An sparse matrix of _p ≈_ 0 _._ 5 will have _≈_ 50% 0’s and _≈_ 50% 1s.

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## 2.1.4 EVALUATION/FITNESS FUNCTION

The fitness of an individual is ranked by determining the sum of each individual in population as given in 1 such that the fittest individual in a generation is given by equation 2.

where _i ∗ c_ is the size of each individual and ind refers to the individual in population.

## 2.1.5 NEXT GENERATION SELECTION
Assuming each iteration is the next generation. In each generation the fit individual is favoured so:

- The fittest individual is passed of as weight to the next generation.

- Every 5 generations or as per the decided elimination frequency, the individual with lowest fitness is discarded from the population.

## 2.2 DISSIPATING GRADIENTS

Magnitude and gradient based pruning approaches are popular in post-training pruning Srivastava et al. (2014), LeCun et al. (1990). It does not make much sense to employ them pre-training becauseof the randomly generated weights. But in order to reduce error, any network aims to target updating weights that influence the results most. Based upon that hypothesis, in each epoch we sum gradients over and eliminate weights whose weights are not getting updated. In equation 3 N is the total number of iterations for an epoch. In equation 4 epsilon is 1e-6 for all experiments.

One consideration in this approach is to not do this for too many epochs which can be only 2 if the image is very monochrome and more than 2 if the gradients are dissipating more slowly. Moreover, once specific weights have reached their optimal learning, their gradients will dissipate and we don’t want to eliminate them.

## 2.3 COMBINATION DROPOUT

Combination dropout is merely combining Kstarts with dissipating gradients. The weights eliminated use both approaches. We fix p for Kstarts to a certain value of minimum sparsity and further eliminate weights that dissipating gradients method will eliminate as well. The approach achieves better performance than either methods.

## 3 DEFINING P-SPARSITY

In a p-sparse network layer approximately p percent of connection between two layers are eliminated. Figure 1 shows a fully connected conventional neural network (figure 1a) and three sparsely connected networks with different values of p (figures 1b, 1c, 1d).

## 3.1 CONNECTIVITY FACTOR

Connectivity factor, p, determines the percentage of connections to be removed between any two layers of the network. For instance if p = 0.0 than the network is be fully connected as shown in figure 1a, if on the opposite extreme, p=1.0, then there will be no connections between two layers of neurons. If p=0.5, figure 1b, only approximately 50% of the connection in the network remain. If p=0.3, figure 1c, approximately 70% of the connection still exist and if p=0.7, figure 1d, a mere 30% of the connections are active between two network layers. In short p determines percentage of 0’s in an individual sparse matrix shown in equation **??** .

## 4 ALGORITHM

The autoencoder, two and three layered neural networks are trained in a standard manner with adam optimizer and batch update.

## 4.1 KSTARTS ALGORITHM

Algorithm 1 gives the kstarts method:

1. We have K number of sparse matrices as shown in equation **??** generated and we call them KI in algorithm 1.

2. Every time the weight W needs to be updated instead of the usual gradient update we add one step further and using the fitness function, pass the fittest individual as the new W which biases the network towards that sparse matrix.

3. Every 5 iterations, the individual with lowest fitness is dropped.

**Algorithm 1:** k Random starts

**input :** Data, params,K,p **output:** W,b initialize W,b; _KI ←_ K individuals with approximately 1-p percent active connections; **for** _maxiterations_ **do** Run Neural Network; Update weights; **if** _One individual in KI is left_ **then** W _←_ individual; **else** W _←_ individual with maximum sum (of weights); **for** _every 5 iterations_ **do** pop individual with minimum sum (of weights) from KI; **end end end**

- 4.2 DISSIPATING GRADIENTS ALGORITHM

Algorithm 2 is more simple and just eliminates weights with sum of gradients equal to zero in the first 1-4 epochs depending on the desired sparsity.

Figure 2: Performance of dropout methods with increasing sparsity.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS AND RESULTS

The experiments performed on two datasets; MNIST Deng (2012) and Fashion MNIST Xiao et al. (2017). The network architectures are two layered Autoencoder (784-128-64), a three layered NN (784-128-100-10) both with sigmoid activation and adam optimization functions.

The Architecture used for learning curves is a single layered NN(784-10).

5

(a) Dataset : Fashion MNIST (b) Dataset : MNIST

Figure 3: Effect of sample size on learning when varying the sparsity of the network with kstarts. Each trial involves running a p-sparse network on a fixed training set of N examples from dataset for 2.5k iterations and is averaged over 10 trials each.

## 5.1 EFFECT OF INCREASING SPARSITY

As sparsity increases, overall performance reduces. Figure 2 shows the behaviour of various dropout methods presented in this paper.

In case of random dropout, it’s indeed a random shot. Either no useful weight is eliminated or multiple crucial weights are eliminated which decides how well does random dropout perform. Kstarts performs slightly better on average with multiple start choices. Depending on how many independent p-sparse networks are present in the network that can learn well, one of them can be identified, given k is large enough and the fitness function is smartly decided by first examining the weights and gradients.

Dissipating gradients works well as long as the network isn’t learning very fast i.e. some weights are being updated in the consequent epochs. It’s also most reliable. Combination works by far the best because it does not only rely on eliminating weights that are not being updated but also uses kstarts. It seems to achieve superior performance as long as p value chosen is a value that kstarts performs well on.

## 5.2 VARIATION IN SAMPLE SIZE

Figures 3a and 3b show relationship of varying sample size to different sparsity values in a single layer NN over 2.5k iterations.

The interesting result here is that isolated from all other factors like number of parameters, hidden units and various design choices, kstarts dropout performs better on a single layer network compared to even a fully connected network. The standard deviation is also lower to fully connected network as well as random dropout. kstarts dropout also learns faster than a fully connected network. For instance, if the iterations for the experiments in figure 3a and 3b are increased, the fully connected network will eventually reach accuracy of the p-sparse network.

## 6 DESIGN PARAMETER CHOICES

There are a number of design parameter choices for the algorithm 1 presented here. Some are explained in detail in this section.

## 6.1 CHOICE OF FITNESS FUNCTION

Since the fitness function determines the individual being passed on to the next generation and the individual being eliminated. We had three choices for choosing the fitness of an individual each with it’s own pros and cons.

6

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

- **Magnitude:** As opted here, we choose population as shown in equation 1 and then select fitness using equation 2. This skews the selection of new weights to the previously selected sparse matrix from KI and therefore, the initial sparse matrix will be propagated forward. This also renders elimination to be pointless it does not matter if 1 or all other matrices in K are eliminated. Furthermore, the sparse matrix is picked awfully early in the experiments i.e. only after first 5 iterations or so and that is not when weights have reached a saturation point.
- **Gradient:** The second choice is to use gradient of the weights to create population as follows:

- _population_ = _δW ∗ SparseMatrices_ (5)

- doing so can have fitness totally dependant on the current update and the new weights are heavily skewed towards the performance of the current iterations which again doesn’t seem that appropriate.
- **Sum of Gradients:** The third option is summing up the gradients for a number of iterations in the manner we do for dissipating gradients 3 and then use those to create population:

Doing so skews the network toward weights that are updated quickly and are increasing.

## 6.2 NO. OF LAYERS AND NO. HIDDEN UNITS

We initially use single layer NN (784-10) to isolate the effects of kstarts algorithm from effects of other parameters that may have a large impact on performance as the size of the network grows. Those parameters i.e. number of hidden layers, regularization choices, types of layers may aid or adversely effect the performance of the algorithm and by performing the experiments on the basic unit of a neural network we were able to concur that the method is effective in it’s own right. After concluding the approach works we tested on three layered NN and Autoencoder.

## 6.3 COST EFFECTIVESNESS

One beneficial feature of pre-training pruning approaches is that the best p-sparse network is quickly identified. There are a number of methods that exploit sparsity of a matrix for quicker multiplication i.e. (Yuster & Zwick, 2005), (Buluc¸ & Gilbert, 2012) which can be used to quickly retrain large networks. Although that is out of scope for our findings.

## 7 DISCUSSION

- 7.1 EFFECT OF K

1. From the experiments done so far lower K ( _≈_ 10) outperforms or at least performs as well as a higher value of K ( _≈_ 100). This can be because the more times a different matrix is chosen by the network, the more times the network has to adopt to learning with only that p-sparse network.

## 7.2 EFFECT OF P

1. A sparse network can outperforms a fully connected network for lower number of iterations and smaller networks.

2. An appropriate value of p, for instance in these experiments _p ≈_ 0 _._ 5, seems to work best for random dropout, kstarts dropout and combination dropout. A poor choice of p can’t seem to be remedied by a better choice of K.

3. p can be thought of as an information limiter. For better learning, if the network is only provided with particular features, it might have an easier time learning the class specific features in different nodes but this only remains a wishful speculation and requires further analysis. Table 1 shows relationship between k,p and no of iterations in a single layer NN (784-10)

## 8 LIMITATIONS

There are a number of limitations to the approach and further investigation is required in a number of domains.
1. The NNs used are single and three layered feed forward networks and autoencoders. CNNs are not experimented upon.
2. Only classification tasks are considered and those on only two datasets: MNIST and Fashion MNIST.
3. Ideally using sparse matrix should make for efficient computation but since the algorithms for that are not used it at this point does not show what the time comparison of the approaches will be.

## 9 CONCLUSIONS AND FINAL REMARKS

We present two methods for pruning weights pre-training or in the first couple of epochs. The comparisons are made against random dropout and both approaches mostly perform better than the random dropout. We provide a combination dropout approach that consistently outperforms other dropout approaches. A lot more analysis of the approach is required on multiple datasets, learning tasks and network architectures but the basic methods seem to be effective.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes two initialization-time pruning methods—KStarts and Dissipating Gradients—with the goal of identifying high-performing sparse subnetworks before or during early training. The core idea of leveraging evolutionary concepts and gradient dynamics at initialization is conceptually interesting, and the combination method shows modest gains over random dropout. However, the work falls short of making a compelling contribution due to limited empirical validation, weak methodological grounding, and lack of comparison with established baselines.

Overview issues:

- No comparison with state-of-the-art initialization pruning methods: The paper claims to advance pruning at initialization but does not compare KStarts or Dissipating Gradients with established methods like SNIP, GraSP, or SynFlow, which are standard benchmarks in this domain. Without such comparisons, it is impossible to assess whether the proposed methods offer any real improvement. The only baseline used is random dropout, which is known to be a weak performer. This omission severely undermines the paper's contribution claim, as the field expects new initialization pruning methods to outperform or at least match these stronger baselines on standard architectures and datasets.

- KStarts lacks a coherent evolutionary algorithm design: The KStarts method is framed as inspired by evolutionary algorithms, but the implementation deviates significantly from standard practices. The 'fitness function' is simply the sum of weights in a masked network (Equation 2), which has no theoretical justification and does not reflect performance or trainability. There is no mutation or crossover, and selection is purely elitist with no exploration. This reduces KStarts to a greedy selection of the mask with largest weight magnitudes—effectively a form of magnitude pruning at initialization, but without justification for why this should identify trainable subnetworks. The methodological link to evolutionary computation is superficial and misleading.

- Dissipating Gradients relies on an unjustified assumption about gradient saturation: The Dissipating Gradients method assumes that weights with small accumulated gradients in early epochs are unimportant and can be pruned. However, this contradicts standard understanding: early gradients can be small due to initialization scale, network depth, or optimization dynamics, not irrelevance. The method risks removing weights that are critical but slowly updated. The paper provides no evidence that early gradient magnitude correlates with long-term importance, nor does it rule out the possibility that the method simply removes poorly initialized weights rather than unimportant ones. This undermines the conceptual foundation of the approach.

- Evaluation is restricted to trivial architectures and datasets: All experiments are conducted on fully connected networks and autoencoders using MNIST and Fashion-MNIST. There are no results on convolutional networks, which are standard in modern pruning research, nor on more challenging datasets like CIFAR-10 or ImageNet subsets. This raises serious concerns about generalizability. The absence of CNNs is particularly problematic given that structured sparsity and spatial connectivity patterns are central to effective pruning in vision models. The paper's conclusions are therefore limited to a narrow and outdated experimental setting, reducing their relevance to current research.

- Sparsity-performance claims are not rigorously evaluated: The paper lacks a systematic ablation of sparsity levels and their impact on final accuracy. Figure 2 shows performance trends but does not report standard deviations or statistical significance. Table 1 shows accuracy for different k and p values, but the results are inconsistent—e.g., K=100 performs worse than K=10, with no explanation. Moreover, the paper does not report the actual sparsity achieved by each method, nor does it clarify whether sparsity is fixed or dynamic. Without precise reporting of sparsity-accuracy trade-offs across multiple runs, the central claims about efficacy cannot be verified.

- Computational cost and scalability are ignored: KStarts requires maintaining and evaluating K separate masks, which imposes a K-fold memory and computation overhead during early training. For large networks or high K, this could be prohibitive. The paper does not report runtime, memory usage, or any analysis of efficiency. Given that one motivation is computational savings, this is a critical omission. Similarly, the Dissipating Gradients method accumulates gradients over multiple epochs, delaying pruning decisions—yet no justification is given for the 1–4 epoch window or its scalability. The methods are presented as efficient, but no evidence supports this claim.

- No comparison with SNIP, GraSP, or SynFlow: The paper claims to advance pruning at initialization but only compares against random dropout, a weak baseline. Established methods like SNIP, GraSP, and SynFlow are standard for this setting and must be included to assess novelty. Without such comparisons on at least MLPs and a simple CNN, the claim of superior performance lacks context. The authors should evaluate KStarts and Dissipating Gradients on MNIST using the same setup as SynFlow and report accuracy at 50% and 80% sparsity to enable direct comparison.

- Missing ablation on CNNs despite stated limitations: The paper explicitly acknowledges not testing on CNNs, yet draws general conclusions about pruning at initialization. For the claims to be credible, they must be validated on a standard architecture like a small VGG or LeNet on CIFAR-10. A single experiment pruning a two-layer CNN (e.g., 32C32-64C64-512-10) with KStarts and Dissipating Gradients would show whether the methods work beyond fully connected nets. Without this, the scope of the contribution remains unproven.

- No implementation details for scaling to larger models: The paper positions its methods as computationally efficient but provides no runtime, memory, or scalability analysis. For KStarts, maintaining K masks imposes a K-fold overhead—this could be prohibitive for large networks. The authors should report wall-clock time and GPU memory usage for K=10 vs K=50 on a single experiment and discuss how one might reduce the cost, e.g., via gradient-based early elimination. Without such discussion, the practical relevance is unclear.

- Undeveloped implication about p-sparse networks outperforming full networks: The paper claims p-sparse networks can outperform fully connected ones, especially at low iteration counts, but this is only shown for a single-layer NN. To support this, the authors should compute the learning curve up to 10k iterations on Fashion-MNIST with a three-layer MLP and show whether the sparse network remains superior or if the full network eventually catches up. This would clarify whether the benefit is transient or structural.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Compare KStarts and Dissipating Gradients with SNIP, GraSP, and SynFlow on at least one convolutional architecture (e.g., VGG or ResNet-20) using CIFAR-10

- Revise the KStarts method to include proper evolutionary operators (mutation, crossover) or reframe it as a mask selection heuristic without invoking evolutionary algorithms

- Provide theoretical or empirical justification for the use of early gradient magnitude as a pruning criterion in Dissipating Gradients

- Report detailed sparsity-accuracy curves with uncertainty estimates across multiple seeds and architectures

- Include computational cost analysis (training time, memory) for all proposed methods relative to baselines

comments.title: Incorrect population definition in Equation 1
comments.quote: $$population = W * K - SparseMatrices \quad (1)$$
comments.explanation: The equation incorrectly defines the population as a single arithmetic expression involving subtraction and multiplication, which does not match the preceding textual description. The text states that the population is formed by multiplying the model weights W with each of the K sparse matrices, implying a set of K matrices: {W ⊙ M₁, W ⊙ M₂, ..., W ⊙ M_K}, where ⊙ denotes element-wise multiplication (assuming 'sparse matrix' refers to a mask). However, the equation writes 'W * K - SparseMatrices', which is mathematically ambiguous and structurally invalid—it suggests subtracting a set of matrices from a scaled version of W, rather than performing K separate masking operations. This misrepresents the intended computation and introduces a formal inconsistency between the description and the equation. The use of '-' instead of a set constructor or indexing implies an algebraic operation that is not defined in context.

comments.title: Fitness function definition is ill-formed and ambiguous
comments.quote:
$$fittest = \arg \max_{ind} \sum_{j=1}^{i*c} ind[j](\text{ of population}) \quad (2)$$

where $i*c$ is the size of each individual and ind refers to the individual in population.
comments.explanation: The expression 'ind[j]( of population)' is mathematically ill-formed: it attempts to index an individual (ind[j]) and then applies a parenthetical phrase '( of population)' as if it were a function or modifier, which has no standard interpretation. The notation suggests that the fitness is the sum over components of each individual, but the term 'i*c' is undefined in this context—earlier text does not define i or c, and their product as a summation bound lacks justification. Moreover, the sum of raw weight values (if that is intended) is an arbitrary fitness metric with no demonstrated link to trainability or performance, especially at initialization. This formulation fails to specify how the mask or weights contribute to the sum—e.g., are unmasked weights included? Is this sum over magnitudes or signed values?

comments.title: Gradient accumulation loop fails to reset accumulator
comments.explanation: The variable `accumulated_dW` is updated across iterations and epochs without initialization at the start of the loop. If `accumulated_dW` retains values from prior epochs, then gradient accumulation compounds over time. For example, if the algorithm runs for 10 epochs but only the first 1–4 are meant to inform pruning, the sum will include irrelevant later gradients. Worse, if `accumulated_dW` is reused across multiple training runs (e.g., in a hyperparameter sweep), it may carry over from previous experiments. The text states pruning is based on 'the first 1-4 epochs', implying a finite accumulation window. To isolate early dynamics, `accumulated_dW` must be initialized to zero before any accumulation begins. If accumulation spans multiple epochs, it should be reset before the first relevant epoch; if epoch-wise analysis is intended, it should be reset at the start of each epoch.

comments.title: In-loop pruning distorts gradient signals
comments.quote: W $\leftarrow$ W*accumulated_dW;
comments.explanation: The weight masking step `W ← W * accumulated_dW` is applied inside the training loop, meaning once a weight is zeroed, it remains inactive for all subsequent iterations. This disrupts the network’s dynamics during training: gradients can no longer flow through pruned connections, so later gradient computations reflect a modified architecture rather than the original unpruned model. The method aims to assess 'importance' via early gradients, but by altering the network mid-training, it creates a self-fulfilling distortion—pruned weights show no activity because they are prevented from participating. This violates the assumption that gradient signals are measured in a fully functional network. A valid importance measure should be derived from an unaltered forward-backward pass. The correct design would accumulate gradients in a separate phase without modifying weights, then apply pruning before training resumes.

comments.title: Gradient threshold ignores sign and magnitude
comments.explanation: The condition `accumulated_dW < 0.0001` performs a direct comparison between a tensor and a scalar, which in most frameworks implies element-wise comparison. However, without taking the absolute value, this condition prunes only small positive gradients (in [0, 0.0001)) while preserving both large positive and all negative gradients—even those with small magnitude (e.g., -1e-5). This creates an asymmetric pruning rule: a gradient of +1e-5 is pruned, but -1e-5 is retained. Since the stated goal is to identify 'dissipating' or negligible gradients—i.e., those near zero in magnitude—the criterion must depend on absolute value. A symmetric threshold using `abs(accumulated_dW) < 0.0001` would correctly capture small-magnitude gradients regardless of sign. The current version introduces a sign-dependent bias that undermines the algorithm's motivation.

comments.title: Misuse of 'generation' in evolutionary context
comments.quote: Each iteration is referred to as a new generation.
comments.explanation: The paper equates each training iteration with a new generation in an evolutionary algorithm, but this is a misapplication of the term. In evolutionary computation, a generation typically refers to a complete cycle of selection, recombination, and mutation applied to a population, resulting in a new population. Here, no selection, mutation, or crossover occurs between iterations—only repeated masking of weights. The population is reconstructed independently each iteration from fixed K sparse matrices and current weights, with no inheritance or evolution across iterations. Thus, calling each iteration a 'generation' falsely implies an evolutionary dynamic that is not present. This misleads readers about the algorithmic mechanism and weakens the claimed connection to evolutionary methods.

comments.title: Fitness function lacks theoretical justification
comments.quote: 2. Every time the weight W needs to be updated instead of the usual gradient update we add one step further and using the fitness function, pass the fittest individual as the new W which biases the network towards that sparse matrix.
comments.explanation: The algorithm selects the 'fittest' individual based on an undefined fitness function, later implied to be the sum of weights. However, no theoretical or empirical justification is provided for why maximizing the sum of weights in a sparse subnetwork at initialization should correlate with trainability. In standard pruning theory, trainable subnetworks are identified via gradient flow or loss sensitivity (e.g., SNIP, GraSP), not raw weight magnitude. Large initial weights may arise from variance scaling (e.g., Kaiming initialization) and do not necessarily indicate importance. Without a proof or citation linking weight sum to subnetwork quality—especially under fixed sparsity—it remains an arbitrary heuristic. This undermines the method's claim to identify 'what to prune' based on principled criteria.

comments.title: Ambiguous and incorrect weight update mechanism
comments.quote: Every time the weight W needs to be updated instead of the usual gradient update we add one step further and using the fitness function, pass the fittest individual as the new W which biases the network towards that sparse matrix.
comments.explanation: The description implies that W is replaced entirely by one of the KI individuals at each update step, bypassing gradient descent. If W is overwritten by a static sparse matrix, then backpropagation has no long-term effect: gradients computed in one step are erased in the next. This breaks the foundation of SGD-based learning. Alternatively, if the 'individual' refers only to a mask applied to W, the mechanism resembles iterative pruning, but this interpretation is not stated. The text fails to specify whether the update is additive, multiplicative, or replacement-based, nor how the selected individual interacts with the current W. Without clarification, the algorithm risks being either non-functional (if gradients are discarded) or misdescribed (if they are preserved).

comments.title: Incorrect definition of p-sparsity
comments.quote: In a p-sparse network layer approximately p percent of connection between two layers are eliminated.
comments.explanation: The statement misdefines p-sparsity. If p percent of connections are eliminated, the network is (100−p)%-sparse, not p-sparse. For example, eliminating 80% of connections results in an 80%-sparse network, not a 20%-sparse one. The quote defines p as the fraction pruned, but standard usage (and the paper’s own figures) treat p as the sparsity level—i.e., the fraction remaining. This creates confusion in interpreting figures and claims. Rewrite the sentence to: 'In a p-sparse network layer, approximately p percent of connections are retained, meaning (100−p) percent are eliminated.' This aligns with conventional usage in the literature and avoids misinterpretation of sparsity levels.

comments.title: Unjustified elimination frequency with no population model
comments.quote: Every 5 generations or as per the decided elimination frequency, the individual with lowest fitness is discarded from the population.
comments.explanation: The rule for discarding the least fit individual every 5 generations introduces an arbitrary elimination schedule without justification. If selection is elitist and only the fittest is preserved each generation, then the population collapses to a single individual unless new variants are introduced via mutation or crossover. Yet no such mechanisms are mentioned. Without diversity-generating operators, repeatedly removing the 'least fit' is meaningless—there may be no variation to begin with. The entire notion of a 'population' is therefore ill-defined in this context. The quoted rule assumes a population of multiple distinct individuals, but the prior rule only propagates one. This creates a logical inconsistency: you cannot eliminate the worst of a population if the population is not maintained.

comments.title: No handling of bias gradients in pruning logic
comments.explanation: The algorithm explicitly accumulates `dW` and applies the mask to weights `W`, but makes no mention of bias terms `b` or their gradients `db`. The input includes `params`, suggesting both weights and biases are present, yet only `dW` is accumulated and used for pruning. Biases often have different gradient scales and dynamics compared to weights, and neglecting them may lead to inconsistent sparsity patterns or unintended behavior (e.g., keeping a zeroed weight but active bias). If the goal is structured pruning or full parameter efficiency, biases should also be considered. Alternatively, if biases are intentionally excluded, this choice must be justified. The current silence on bias treatment creates ambiguity in implementation and reproducibility.

comments.title: Fitness function undermines evolutionary dynamics
comments.quote: - Magnitude: As opted here, we choose population as shown in equation 1 and then select fitness using equation 2. This skews the selection of new weights to the previously selected sparse matrix from KI and therefore, the initial sparse matrix will be propagated forward. This also renders elimination to be pointless it does not matter if 1 or all other matrices in K are eliminated. Furthermore, the sparse matrix is picked awfully early in the experiments i.e. only after first 5 iterations or so and that is not when weights have reached a saturation point.
comments.explanation: The authors explicitly state that using weight magnitude causes the initial sparse mask to dominate all future generations, making elimination ineffective. This implies that once a mask is selected early, it propagates unchanged—effectively halting evolution. In evolutionary algorithms, selection pressure must balance exploitation and exploration; here, the fitness function creates a runaway feedback loop favoring the first successful individual. If the initial mask is suboptimal (which is likely within 5 iterations), the population cannot recover. The paper provides no mechanism—such as mutation, diversity maintenance, or fitness normalization—to counteract this. Thus, the chosen fitness function contradicts the core purpose of an evolutionary search.

comments.title: Gradient-based population definition is ill-posed
comments.quote:
- Gradient: The second choice is to use gradient of the weights to create population as follows:

$$population = \delta W * SparseMatrices \quad (5)$$

doing so can have fitness totally dependant on the current update and the new weights are heavily skewed towards the performance of the current iterations which again doesn’t seem that appropriate.
comments.explanation: The expression $\delta W * \text{SparseMatrices}$ is mathematically ambiguous. $\delta W$ is a single tensor of gradients at one step, while $\text{SparseMatrices}$ denotes a set of $K$ distinct binary masks. Element-wise multiplication (implied by $*$) cannot be applied between a single tensor and a set of matrices unless the operation is defined per mask. Even then, forming the 'population' as $\delta W \odot M_k$ for each mask $M_k$ does not yield a population of networks or weights—it yields gradient-scaled masks, which are not valid individuals in a genetic algorithm. A population should consist of candidate solutions (e.g., pruned weight matrices), not transformations of gradients. The paper fails to specify how these objects become individuals subject to selection and crossover.

comments.title: Sum of gradients misused in population generation
comments.quote:
- Sum of Gradients: The third option is summing up the gradients for a number of iterations in the manner we do for dissipating gradients 3 and then use those to create population:

$$population = (\sum \delta W) * SparseMatrices \quad (6)$$

Doing so skews the network toward weights that are updated quickly and are increasing.
comments.explanation: Equation (6) repeats the flaw in equation (5): $(\sum \delta W)$ is a cumulative gradient tensor, not a per-individual measure. Multiplying it element-wise with each sparse matrix does not constitute a population update—it merely produces scaled versions of the masks, not evolved networks. There is no mechanism described for converting these into new individuals or updating the population. Moreover, favoring weights with large cumulative gradients assumes that high update magnitude correlates with importance, but this conflates transient signal with structural significance. For example, noisy or unstable gradients early in training may inflate certain weights' scores without reflecting their long-term utility. The method offers no normalization or statistical validation to mitigate this.

comments.title: Insufficient training details for reproducibility
comments.quote: The autoencoder, two and three layered neural networks are trained in a standard manner with adam optimizer and batch update.
comments.explanation: The statement provides no concrete details about the training procedure, such as learning rate, batch size, number of epochs, or convergence criteria. While 'standard manner' is vague and context-dependent, even basic settings like Adam's β₁, β₂, or ϵ are not specified. This lack of detail makes it impossible to reproduce the results or verify whether the pruning methods interact fairly with the optimizer. For example, early gradient accumulation in Dissipating Gradients is sensitive to optimization dynamics, yet no information is given about how quickly the network updates in early iterations. The sentence must specify the actual hyperparameters used.

comments.title: Incorrect sparsity matrix terminology
comments.quote: Each individual, in a population of K instances, is a sparse matrix of size equal to the size of network weights, W. The number of 0’s and 1’s in the sparsity matrix are determined by the connectivity factor p which is further described in section 3. An sparse matrix of $p\approx 0.5$ will have $\approx 50\%$ 0’s and $\approx 50\%$ 1s.
comments.explanation: The term 'sparse matrix' is misused here. A matrix with $p \approx 0.5$ is not sparse—sparse matrices are defined by having a majority of zero entries, typically much less than 50%. The paper uses 'sparse matrix' to describe the mask, but with $p=0.5$, half the entries are ones, which contradicts the standard definition. This creates confusion between structural sparsity and balanced binary masks. The mask is better described as a binary selection matrix or pruning mask, not a sparse matrix. The misuse persists throughout the paper and undermines precise communication, especially in a pruning context where sparsity level is a core metric.

comments.title: Combination method lacks formal definition
comments.quote: Combination dropout is merely combining Kstarts with dissipating gradients. The weights eliminated use both approaches. We fix p for Kstarts to a certain value of minimum sparsity and further eliminate weights that dissipating gradients method will eliminate as well. The approach achieves better performance than either methods.
comments.explanation: The section describes 'combination dropout' as merging KStarts and dissipating gradients, but provides no formal definition or pseudocode for how the two masks are combined—whether union, intersection, or sequential pruning. The phrase 'further eliminate weights' suggests a sequential application, but it is unclear whether KStarts is applied first or vice versa, and whether the sparsity constraint p applies before or after gradient-based pruning. This ambiguity makes the method unreplicable. For example, if KStarts already prunes to sparsity p, subsequent gradient-based elimination would violate that sparsity unless p is a lower bound. The claim that the combination 'achieves better performance' is unsupported by any data in this section.

comments.title: Contradictory claim about retraining irrelevance
comments.quote: Retraining of the model is neither necessary nor affects the performance of the p-sparse network.
comments.explanation: The claim that retraining 'does not affect the performance' of the p-sparse network contradicts the core premise of pruning at initialization, which assumes that early training dynamics inform which subnetwork to keep. If retraining has no effect, then the pruning decision is irrelevant—any mask would perform equally well, which invalidates the need for KStarts or Dissipating Gradients. Conversely, if the pruning step matters, then retraining must affect performance. This logical inconsistency suggests a misunderstanding of the pruning process. The statement likely intends to say that the pruned network trains efficiently without fine-tuning, but as written, it implies a structural flaw in the method's design.

comments.title: Unjustified superiority claim for combination method
comments.quote: 3) Combination of dissipating gradients and kstarts outperforms either methods and random dropout consistently.
comments.explanation: The abstract asserts that the combination method 'consistently outperforms' the individual methods and random dropout, but no evidence is presented in this section to support this claim. There are no results, citations to later sections, or theoretical arguments justifying why combining two conceptually distinct methods—population-based selection and gradient-based pruning—should yield superior performance. In fact, the two methods operate on different principles: KStarts selects among sparse masks, while Dissipating Gradients modifies connectivity based on weight change. Their combination is not obviously synergistic. Without empirical validation or mechanistic justification, this claim is speculative and overreaches the available support.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of prior work on sparse networks
comments.quote: Moreover, it appears to be the case that when isolated from other factors sparse networks outperform fully connected networks. When not isolated they perform at least as well up to a percentage of sparsity depending on the number of parameters in the said network.
comments.explanation: This claim misrepresents the findings of Frankle and Carbin (2018), which the paper cites as support. The Lottery Ticket Hypothesis shows that *some* sparse subnetworks can match full-network performance when retrained, not that sparse networks generally outperform or even match them out of the box. The statement implies broad empirical support for sparse networks being superior, which is not justified. The phrase 'it appears to be the case' is too weak to correct for this overstatement. Rewrite the sentence to reflect that only specific, carefully identified subnetworks (like winning tickets) show this behavior, not sparse networks in general.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes two methods for pruning neural networks at initialization (KStarts and dissipating gradients) but suffers from significant methodological flaws, limited experimental validation, and questionable theoretical grounding. The core issues include an ill-defined fitness function for evolutionary selection, inconsistent algorithmic descriptions, and insufficient evaluation against established baselines in the pruning-at-initialization literature.

The paper attempts to address the important problem of pruning at initialization but presents methods with unclear theoretical justification and inadequate experimental validation. The KStarts evolutionary approach uses a problematic fitness function based on weight magnitudes, which contradicts the paper's own critique of magnitude-based pruning. The dissipating gradients method eliminates weights too aggressively based on arbitrary thresholds. Experiments are limited to simple architectures on MNIST datasets without comparison to state-of-the-art initialization pruning methods like SNIP, GraSP, or SynFlow.

Overview issues:

- KStarts Fitness Function Contradicts Problem Premise: The KStarts method uses weight magnitude as its fitness function (Equation 2), selecting individuals with the highest sum of weights. This directly contradicts the paper's own premise that weight magnitudes at initialization are poor indicators of importance (Section 2.2 criticizes magnitude-based pruning). Using magnitude as fitness will propagate larger random initial weights rather than identifying truly useful connections. The authors acknowledge this issue in Section 6.1, stating it 'renders elimination pointless' and 'skews selection to the previously selected sparse matrix,' yet they proceed with this approach anyway. A proper fitness function should incorporate gradient information or loss sensitivity, not raw weight magnitudes.

- Dissipating Gradients Eliminates Weights Prematurely: The dissipating gradients method (Equations 3-4, Algorithm 2) eliminates weights with accumulated gradients below epsilon=1e-6 after only 1-4 epochs. This threshold is arbitrary and dangerous - important weights may have small gradients early in training while still being crucial for final performance. The method risks eliminating connections that would become important later, especially in deeper networks where gradient flow takes time to establish. The paper provides no theoretical justification for why early gradient magnitude correlates with final importance, and the choice of 1e-6 appears to be an unstated hyperparameter requiring tuning.

- Missing Comparison to State-of-the-Art Pruning-at-Initialization Methods: The experiments only compare against random dropout, ignoring established PaI methods like SNIP, GraSP, and SynFlow that are specifically designed for this problem. The literature context (Frankle et al., 2020) shows these methods are the proper baselines, yet Table 1 and Figures 2-3 contain no comparison to them. Without demonstrating superiority over existing PaI techniques, the paper's claims of better performance are unsubstantiated. The methods should be evaluated against SNIP, GraSP, and SynFlow on the same architectures and datasets to establish their relative efficacy.

- Inconsistent Algorithm Descriptions and Implementation Details: Algorithm 1 contains contradictions: it states 'if One individual in KI is left then W ← individual' but also 'else W ← individual with maximum sum', creating ambiguity about weight updates. Algorithm 2 has pseudocode that doesn't match Equation 4's logic - it sets accumulated_dW to 0 or 1 rather than using it as a mask. The population definition in Equation 1 multiplies weights by sparse matrices, but it's unclear how this creates K distinct individuals or how they evolve. These inconsistencies make reproduction impossible and cast doubt on the experimental results.

- Limited Experimental Scope and Generalization Claims: The paper tests only on simple feedforward networks and autoencoders with MNIST/Fashion-MNIST datasets (Section 5). CNNs, transformers, or more complex datasets are absent despite being standard in pruning literature. The claim that 'sparse networks outperform fully connected networks' (Section 5.2) is only shown for single-layer networks on toy datasets. The limitations section acknowledges this gap but doesn't address it experimentally. Without testing on modern architectures or larger datasets, the method's generalization remains unproven.

- Statistical Significance and Reproducibility Concerns: Table 1 reports results averaged over 10 runs but provides no statistical significance tests. The standard deviations are large (e.g., 50.53 ± 10.7 for k=10, p=0.5 at 100 iterations), suggesting high variability. The evolutionary KStarts method's sensitivity to random seeds is not analyzed, yet Section 6.1 notes fitness depends heavily on early iterations. Without proper statistical validation and seed stability analysis, the reported performance advantages may not be reliable.

- Missing worked example of p-sparsity computation: The paper defines p-sparsity conceptually but never demonstrates how to compute it for a concrete network. Readers need to see how the sparse matrices are actually generated and applied to a specific layer. The authors should work through the p-sparsity calculation for their 784-128-64 autoencoder, showing the exact sparse matrices created for p=0.5 and how they transform the weight matrices W1 and W2. This would clarify the implementation and help readers understand the computational overhead.

- No demonstration of non-vacuous results: The paper claims its methods find better sparse networks but never shows a concrete example where the identified sparse network actually outperforms alternatives. A complete paper should include at least one fully-specified experiment showing: (1) the initial random weights, (2) the sparse mask found by KStarts, (3) the final trained performance, and (4) comparison against both random dropout and a fully connected baseline trained from the same initialization. Without such a worked case, readers cannot verify that the methods are doing more than random chance.

- Missing computational cost analysis: The paper claims pruning at initialization reduces computational cost but provides no timing comparisons or FLOPs analysis. Section 6.3 mentions sparse matrix multiplication algorithms but doesn't measure actual speedups. The authors should report training time per epoch for each method on their architectures, showing the overhead of maintaining K sparse matrices in KStarts and any savings from sparsity. Without concrete cost measurements, the computational efficiency claims remain unsubstantiated.

- Underdeveloped gradient dissipation theory: The dissipating gradients method assumes weights with small early gradients are unimportant, but provides no theoretical or empirical justification. The paper should include an analysis showing correlation between early gradient magnitudes and final weight importance, perhaps by tracking a sample of weights throughout training. This would justify why eliminating weights with accumulated gradients below epsilon after 1-4 epochs is valid. Without this foundation, the method appears heuristic and potentially harmful.

Recommendation: reject

Revision targets:

- Replace the magnitude-based fitness function with a theoretically justified alternative (e.g., gradient sensitivity or loss-based importance)

- Add comprehensive comparisons against SNIP, GraSP, and SynFlow baselines on the same experimental setups

- Fix algorithmic inconsistencies and provide complete, reproducible pseudocode for both methods

- Extend experiments to CNN architectures and larger datasets beyond MNIST to demonstrate generalization

- Conduct statistical significance testing and analyze random seed sensitivity for the evolutionary approach

comments.title: Algorithm 2 matrix-scalar comparison error
comments.explanation: The pseudocode contains a fundamental type error. accumulated_dW is accumulated as accumulated_dW ← accumulated_dW + dW, where dW has the same dimensions as weight matrix W. Therefore accumulated_dW is a matrix, but the comparison "if accumulated_dW < 0.0001" treats it as a scalar. This comparison is mathematically invalid. The algorithm should either use a norm (e.g., ||accumulated_dW|| < 0.0001) or element-wise comparison. Rewrite the conditional as "if ||accumulated_dW||_F < 0.0001 then" to compare the Frobenius norm, or "if all(abs(accumulated_dW) < 0.0001) then" for element-wise comparison.

comments.title: Missing accumulated_dW initialization
comments.explanation: The algorithm uses accumulated_dW in the accumulation operation without initialization. This would cause undefined behavior on the first iteration. Add "accumulated_dW ← zeros(size(W));" after "initialize W,b;" to properly initialize the accumulator matrix.

comments.title: Contradiction between gradient dissipation rationale and elimination rule
comments.quote: Moreover, once specific weights have reached their optimal learning, their gradients will dissipate and we don’t want to eliminate them.
comments.explanation: This statement directly contradicts the method's core elimination rule in Equation 4. The method eliminates weights with accumulated gradients below epsilon, but the authors acknowledge that weights with dissipated gradients (which would have small accumulated values) should NOT be eliminated because they may have reached optimal learning. This creates a fundamental logical inconsistency: the method eliminates exactly the weights the authors claim should be preserved. The authors need to either modify the elimination criterion or reconcile this contradiction.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 population initialization contradicts p-sparsity definition
comments.quote: \gets K$ individuals with approximately 1-p percent active connections;
comments.explanation: The initialization description contains a critical mathematical error. If p represents the sparsity fraction (e.g., p=0.8 means 80% sparse), then the proportion of active connections should be (1-p), not '1-p percent'. For p=0.8, the current description would yield 0.2% active connections instead of the intended 20%. Rewrite '$KI← K$ individuals with approximately 1-p percent active connections;' as '$KI← K$ individuals with approximately (1-p)×100 percent active connections;' to correctly represent the sparsity level.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 contains contradictory weight update logic
comments.quote: if One individual in KI is left then W $\leftarrow$ individual; else W $\leftarrow$ individual with maximum sum (of weights); f
comments.explanation: The algorithm's conditional logic is indeed contradictory. When only one individual remains in KI, both conditions are satisfied: the 'if' condition triggers because exactly one individual remains, and the 'else' condition would also select that same individual since it trivially has the maximum sum. This creates logical redundancy and ambiguity about the intended behavior. The algorithm should either remove the special case for single individuals or clarify that the maximum sum selection applies universally. Rewrite the conditional as 'W ← individual with maximum sum (of weights)' without the special case, since the maximum sum rule already handles the single-individual case correctly.

comments.title: Logical contradiction in chosen method
comments.quote: - Magnitude: As opted here, we choose population as shown in equation 1 and then select fitness using equation 2. This skews the selection of new weights to the previously selected sparse matrix from KI and therefore, the initial sparse matrix will be propagated forward. This also renders elimination to be pointless it does not matter if 1 or all other matrices in K are eliminated.
comments.explanation: The authors acknowledge that their chosen magnitude-based fitness function 'renders elimination to be pointless' and 'skews the selection' yet proceed with it anyway. This is a fundamental methodological flaw. If elimination is pointless, then the evolutionary algorithm reduces to simply selecting the initial random weights with highest magnitude, which contradicts the entire premise of finding optimal sparse structures. The authors should either fix this flaw or choose a different fitness function that doesn't negate the evolutionary mechanism.

comments.title: Ambiguous matrix multiplication notation in population equation
comments.quote: $$population = W * K_{SparseMatrices} \quad (1)$$
comments.explanation: Equation (1) uses ambiguous notation. The right side shows W multiplied by K_{SparseMatrices}, but the text describes multiplying W with 'every instance' of the K sparse matrices to create a 'set of matrices'. The equation should explicitly show this as a set or collection, not a single multiplication. It should be written as population = {W * S_i | i = 1..K} where S_i are the individual sparse matrices.

comments.title: Undefined variables in fitness function
comments.quote:
$$fittest = \arg \max_{ind} \sum_{j=1}^{i*c} ind[j] (\text{of population}) \quad (2)$$

where $i*c$ is the size of each individual and ind refers to the individual in population.
comments.explanation: The equation uses variables $i$ and $c$ in the term $i*c$ but neither variable is defined in the explanation. The text only states that $i*c$ is the size of each individual, but doesn't explain what $i$ and $c$ represent individually. This makes the fitness function ambiguous and impossible to implement without additional context.

comments.title: Ambiguous sparse matrix definition
comments.quote: Each individual, in a population of K instances, is a sparse matrix of size equal to the size of network weights, W. The number of 0’s and 1’s in the sparsity matrix are determined by the connectivity factor p which is further described in section 3. An sparse matrix of $p\approx 0.5$ will have $\approx 50\%$ 0’s and $\approx 50\%$ 1s.
comments.explanation: The description of the sparse matrix is mathematically inconsistent. A sparse matrix typically contains mostly zeros with few non-zero values, but here the authors describe a matrix with approximately 50% zeros and 50% ones for p≈0.5. This is not a sparse matrix by standard definition - it's a moderately dense binary mask. The authors should clarify whether they mean (1) a binary mask matrix applied element-wise to W, or (2) an actual sparse matrix representation. If it's a binary mask, they should not call it a 'sparse matrix' as this creates confusion with standard sparse matrix terminology where sparsity refers to the proportion of zero elements.

comments.title: Connectivity factor definition contradiction
comments.quote: Connectivity factor, p, determines the percentage of connections to be removed between any two layers of the network. For instance if p = 0.0 than the network is be fully connected as shown in figure 1a, if on the opposite extreme, p=1.0, then there will be no connections between two layers of neurons. If p=0.5, figure 1b, only approximately $50\%$ of the connection in the network remain. If p=0.3, figure 1c, approximately $70\%$ of the connection still exist and if p=0.7, figure 1d, a mere $30\%$ of the connections are active between two network layers. In short p determines percentage of 0’s in an individual sparse matrix shown in equation 2.
comments.explanation: The text contains a logical contradiction in defining the connectivity factor p. It first states that p 'determines the percentage of connections to be removed' but then gives examples where p represents the percentage of connections that REMAIN. For p=0.5, it says '50% of the connection remain' which contradicts the initial definition of p as removal percentage. Similarly, for p=0.3 it says '70% of the connection still exist' and for p=0.7 it says '30% of the connections are active'. The final sentence clarifies that p 'determines percentage of 0's in an individual sparse matrix' which aligns with the removal interpretation. The examples are mathematically inconsistent with this definition. Rewrite the examples to match the definition: for p=0.5, state that 50% of connections are removed (so 50% remain); for p=0.3, state that 30% are removed (so 70% remain); for p=0.7, state that 70% are removed (so 30% remain).

comments.title: Inconsistent network architecture description
comments.quote:
The network architectures are two layered Autoencoder (784-128-64), a three layered NN (784-128-100-10) both with sigmoid activation and adam optimization functions.

The Architecture used for learning curves is a single layered NN(784-10).
comments.explanation: The text describes the main architectures as a two-layer autoencoder and three-layer neural network, but then states the learning curves use a single-layer NN. This creates confusion because Figure 3 shows learning curves but doesn't specify which architecture they correspond to. The caption mentions 'kstarts' and 'p-sparse network' but doesn't clarify whether this refers to the single-layer NN or one of the previously described architectures. This inconsistency makes it impossible to determine which experimental setup produced the results in Figure 3. The authors should explicitly state which architecture was used for the learning curve experiments in Figure 3.

comments.title: Missing experimental parameters in Figure 3
comments.quote: Figure 3: Effect of sample size on learning when varying the sparsity of the network with kstarts. Each trial involves running a p-sparse network on a fixed training set of N examples from dataset for 2.5k iterations and is averaged over 10 trials each.
comments.explanation: The caption states experiments run for '2.5k iterations' but doesn't specify the learning rate, batch size, or other key hyperparameters that affect learning curves. More critically, it mentions 'varying the sparsity' but the figure axes only show sample size (N) and accuracy, with no indication of which sparsity levels were tested. The different colored lines in the figure likely represent different sparsity levels, but this isn't explained in the caption. Without knowing what each line represents, readers cannot interpret the results or understand how sparsity affects the relationship between sample size and learning performance.

comments.title: Logical contradiction in learning speed claim
comments.quote: kstarts dropout also learns faster than a fully connected network. For instance, if the iterations for the experiments in figure 3a and 3b are increased, the fully connected network will eventually reach accuracy of the p-sparse network.
comments.explanation: These two statements contradict each other. The first claims kstarts dropout learns faster, but the second example shows the fully connected network eventually catching up to the p-sparse network's accuracy. If the fully connected network reaches the same accuracy given more iterations, then kstarts doesn't have a fundamental learning advantage - it may just reach a plateau faster due to having fewer parameters to optimize. The authors should clarify whether they mean 'reaches final accuracy faster' or 'converges to a different local optimum faster'. The current wording is misleading.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated performance claim without evidence
comments.quote: Combination dropout is merely combining Kstarts with dissipating gradients. The weights eliminated use both approaches. We fix p for Kstarts to a certain value of minimum sparsity and further eliminate weights that dissipating gradients method will eliminate as well. The approach achieves better performance than either methods.
comments.explanation: The final sentence claims the combination approach achieves better performance than either individual method, but provides no supporting evidence, data, or reference to results elsewhere in the paper. This is a significant empirical claim that requires verification through experimental results, statistical tests, or at minimum a citation to where this comparison is presented. Without evidence, this claim is unsubstantiated and should either be removed or supported with concrete results showing the performance comparison.

comments.title: Abstract claims three approaches but only describes two
comments.quote:
training losses.

In approbation of the above reasons, the paper presents two approaches to prune at initialization. The goal is to achieve higher sparsity while preserving performance. 1) K-starts, begins with k random p-sparse matrices at initialization. In the first couple of epochs the network then determines the ”fittest” of these p-sparse matrices in an attempt to find the ”lottery ticket” (((Frankle and Carbin, 2018))) p-sparse network. The approach is adopted from how evolutionary algorithms find the best individual. Depending on the Neural Network architecture, fitness criteria can be based on magnitude of network weights, magnitude of gradient accumulation over an epoch or a combination of both. 2) Dissipating gradients approach, aims at eliminating weights that remain within a fraction of their initial value during the first couple of epochs. Removing weights in this manner despite their magnitude best preserves performance of the network. Contrarily, the approach also takes the most epochs to achieve higher sparsity. 3) Combination of dissipating gradients and kstarts outperforms either methods and random dropout consistently.
comments.explanation: The abstract states it presents 'two approaches' but then describes three distinct methods (K-starts, Dissipating gradients, and their Combination). This is a clear logical contradiction - either the paper presents two approaches (where the combination is not a separate approach) or three approaches. The enumeration '1)', '2)', '3)' clearly indicates three methods are being presented. Rewrite the first sentence as 'the paper presents three approaches to prune at initialization' to match the enumerated list.

comments.title: Double parentheses in citation formatting
comments.quote: zation. In the first couple of epochs the network then determines the ”fittest” of these p-sparse matrices in an attempt to find the ”lottery ticket” (((Frankle and Carbin, 2018))) p-sparse network.
comments.explanation: The citation contains triple parentheses '(((Frankle and Carbin, 2018)))' which appears to be a formatting error. Standard academic citation format would be '(Frankle and Carbin, 2018)'. This should be corrected to maintain proper formatting throughout the paper.

comments.title: Contradictory claim about epochs for dissipating gradients
comments.quote: 2) Dissipating gradients approach, aims at eliminating weights that remain within a fraction of their initial value during the first couple of epochs. Removing weights in this manner despite their magnitude best preserves performance of the network. Contrarily, the approach also takes the most epochs to achieve higher sparsity.
comments.explanation: There's a logical contradiction in describing the dissipating gradients approach. It first states the method works 'during the first couple of epochs' but then claims 'the approach also takes the most epochs to achieve higher sparsity.' If it only operates during the first couple of epochs, how can it take the most epochs overall? This needs clarification - either the method continues beyond the initial epochs, or the claim about taking 'the most epochs' is incorrect relative to other methods.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about avoiding retraining losses
comments.quote: Furthermore, it handles overfitting just as well as post training dropout. It is also averse to retraining losses.
comments.explanation: The claim that initialization pruning is 'averse to retraining losses' is unsubstantiated in the abstract. Since the paper explicitly states 'Retraining of the model is neither necessary' in the benefits section, it's unclear what 'retraining losses' refers to. If retraining isn't performed, there can't be retraining losses to avoid. This appears to be either a meaningless claim or a confusion about what 'retraining losses' means. The authors should either remove this claim or clarify what specific type of losses they're referring to.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of dropout as pruning method
comments.quote: The post-training pruning has several approaches in place such as adding various regularization schemes to prune the network Louizos et al. (2017); Pan et al. (2016) or using second derivative or hessian of the weights for dropout LeCun et al. (1990); Hassibi and Stork (1993). Han et al. (2015); Alford et al. (2019); Zhu and Gupta (2017) use an efficient iterative pruning method to iteratively increase sparsity. Srivastava et al. (2014) dropout random hidden units with p probability instead of weights to avoid overfitting in general.
comments.explanation: The text incorrectly characterizes dropout as a pruning method and misattributes the citations. Dropout (Srivastava et al., 2014) is a regularization technique that randomly deactivates units during training, not a pruning method that permanently removes weights. The citations to LeCun et al. (1990) and Hassibi & Stork (1993) are about optimal brain surgeon and second-order methods for pruning, not dropout. This creates confusion about the relationship between regularization and pruning. The sentence should separate regularization methods from actual pruning approaches and correctly attribute each technique to its proper citation.

comments.title: Contradictory claim about sparse network performance
comments.quote: Moreover, it appears to be the case that when isolated from other factors sparse networks outperform fully connected networks. When not isolated they perform at least as well up to a percentage of sparsity depending on the number of parameters in the said network.
comments.explanation: This claim contradicts the paper's own experimental scope and the broader literature. The paper only tests on simple feedforward networks and autoencoders with MNIST/Fashion-MNIST, yet makes a general claim about sparse networks outperforming fully connected ones. The literature context shows that pruning-at-initialization methods typically underperform fully connected networks, with Frankle et al. (2020) demonstrating that per-weight decisions are often no better than random. The claim should be qualified to reflect the limited experimental settings actually tested in the paper rather than stated as a general principle.

comments.title: Inconsistent citation of Lottery Ticket Hypothesis
comments.quote:
approaches exploit the knowledge that a network has multiple underlying p-sparse networks that perform just as well and in some cases even better when contrasted with their fully connected

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

counterparts (Frankle and Carbin, 2018). Wha
comments.explanation: The citation to Frankle and Carbin (2018) is incorrect. The Lottery Ticket Hypothesis paper was published in 2019 at ICLR, not 2018. More importantly, the hypothesis states that dense networks contain sparse subnetworks that can match the performance of the original network WHEN TRAINED FROM THE SAME INITIALIZATION, not that sparse networks generally outperform fully connected ones. The paper's claim about sparse networks performing 'even better' than fully connected counterparts goes beyond what the Lottery Ticket Hypothesis actually demonstrates. The citation should be corrected to (Frankle and Carbin, 2019) and the claim should be aligned with the actual findings of that work.

comments.title: Vague population definition creates implementation ambiguity
comments.quote: Population: A product of network weights and sparse matrices.
comments.explanation: The population definition is mathematically ambiguous. 'Product' could mean element-wise multiplication, matrix multiplication, or other operations. Without specifying the exact operation and dimensions, readers cannot implement or verify the algorithm. The paper should define the population as P = {W ⊙ S_i} for i=1..K where ⊙ denotes element-wise multiplication, W are the network weights, and S_i are K different sparse matrices.

comments.title: Individual definition is circular and uninformative
comments.quote: Individual: An instance of the population.
comments.explanation: This definition is circular - an individual cannot be defined as an instance of the population when the population itself is defined as a collection of individuals. In evolutionary algorithms, an individual should be explicitly defined as a specific weight configuration with its associated sparse matrix. The authors should define an individual as I_i = W ⊙ S_i where S_i is one of the K sparse matrices.

comments.title: Missing fitness function specification contradicts evolutionary algorithm requirements
comments.quote: Fitness Function: The heuristic chosen for evaluation of the population.
comments.explanation: The fitness function is mentioned but never specified in this section, yet it's essential for evolutionary algorithms to perform selection. Without defining the actual heuristic (e.g., weight magnitude sum, loss on validation set, gradient sensitivity), the algorithm cannot be implemented. This omission is particularly problematic given the paper-level issue that the fitness function contradicts the premise about weight magnitudes.

comments.title: Undefined equation reference in population definition
comments.quote: We first initialize K sparse matrices, a single instance of these K sparse matrices can be seen in equation ??
comments.explanation: The text references 'equation ??' which is undefined. This creates ambiguity about where readers should look for the specific sparse matrix instance. The authors should either provide the actual equation number or include the sparse matrix definition directly in this section.

comments.title: Inconsistent population definition between text and equation
comments.quote: The resulting set of matrices is our population for that iteration. Each iteration is referred to as a new generation.
comments.explanation: The text states the population is a 'set of matrices' but Equation (1) defines population as a single matrix product. This inconsistency needs resolution. Either the equation should show the population as a set/collection of matrices, or the text should clarify that population refers to a concatenated/aggregated form of the K matrix products.

comments.title: Equation numbering inconsistency
comments.quote:
The fitness of an individual is ranked by determining the sum of each individual in population as given in 1 such that the fittest individual in a generation is given by equation 1.

$$fittest = \arg \max_{ind} \sum_{j=1}^{i*c} ind[j] (\text{of population}) \quad (2)$$
comments.explanation: The text states the fitness function is "given in 1" and "given by equation 1", but the actual equation is labeled as equation (2). This creates confusion about which equation number to reference. The text should consistently refer to equation (2) since that's the actual equation label shown.

comments.title: Ambiguous summation notation
comments.quote: $$fittest = \arg \max_{ind} \sum_{j=1}^{i*c} ind[j] (\text{of population}) \quad (2)$$
comments.explanation: The notation $ind[j]$ suggests that an individual is being indexed by $j$, but it's unclear what structure $ind$ has. If $ind$ represents a weight matrix or vector, the indexing should be clarified. Additionally, the parenthetical "(of population)" is mathematically vague - it should specify whether the maximization is over all individuals in the population or if there's some other constraint.

comments.title: Evolutionary algorithm terminology inconsistency
comments.quote:
Assuming each iteration is the next generation. In each generation the fit individual is favoured so:

- The fittest individual is passed of as weight to the next generation.
- Every 5 generations or as per the decided elimination frequency, the individual with lowest fitness is discarded from the population.
comments.explanation: The description contradicts standard evolutionary algorithm terminology and logic.
