The text states 'the fittest individual is passed of as weight to the next generation' but then says 'the individual with lowest fitness is discarded'. This creates ambiguity about whether both selection and elimination happen each generation, or if elimination only occurs every 5 generations. Additionally, 'passed of as weight' is unclear - it should specify whether the fittest individual's weights are copied directly, used for crossover, or serve as a parent. The elimination frequency of 'every 5 generations or as per the decided elimination frequency' is also contradictory - either it's fixed at 5 or it's configurable. Rewrite this section to clearly specify: (1) what happens to the fittest individual each generation, (2) whether elimination occurs every 5 generations or at a configurable frequency, and (3) the exact mechanism for weight transfer between generations.

comments.title: Gradient accumulation equation missing iteration index
comments.quote: $$Accumulated\_dw = \sum_{i=1}^{N} dW \quad (3)$$
comments.explanation: Equation 3 is mathematically incorrect as written. The summation should accumulate gradients over iterations, but dW lacks an iteration index. The correct formulation should be Accumulated_dw = \sum_{i=1}^{N} dW_i, where dW_i represents the gradient at iteration i. Without the subscript, the equation suggests summing the same gradient N times, which would just multiply by N rather than accumulate the actual gradient history across iterations.

comments.title: Missing Equation 1 reference in gradient accumulation explanation
comments.quote: Based upon that hypothesis, in each epoch we sum gradients over and eliminate weights whose weights are not getting updated. In equation 1 N is the total number of iterations for an epoch.
comments.explanation: The text references Equation 1 for defining N, but Equation 1 is not provided in this section. The equations shown are labeled (3) and (4). Either Equation 1 is missing from this section, or the reference is incorrect. Since the authors specifically state 'In equation 1 N is the total number of iterations for an epoch,' this missing equation creates ambiguity about a key parameter definition.

comments.title: Ambiguous method description lacks implementation details
comments.quote: We fix p for Kstarts to a certain value of minimum sparsity and further eliminate weights that dissipating gradients method will eliminate as well.
comments.explanation: This description is too vague to be reproducible. It doesn't specify: (1) what constitutes a 'minimum sparsity' value or how it's determined, (2) the order of operations (whether Kstarts pruning happens first, then dissipating gradients, or vice versa), (3) how conflicts between the two methods are resolved when they disagree on a weight's importance, and (4) whether the 'minimum sparsity' is a hyperparameter that needs tuning. The method needs a precise algorithmic description to be implementable.

comments.title: Contradiction with paper's own critique of magnitude-based pruning
comments.quote: Combination dropout is merely combining Kstarts with dissipating gradients.
comments.explanation: The paper's overview notes that KStarts uses weight magnitude as its fitness function, which contradicts the paper's premise that weight magnitudes at initialization are poor indicators of importance. By combining KStarts with dissipating gradients without addressing this fundamental contradiction, the combination method inherits the same flaw. The section should acknowledge this inconsistency or explain why combining a magnitude-based approach with gradient-based information resolves the contradiction.

comments.title: Figure caption lacks methodological context
comments.quote: Figure 1: Sample P-Sparse Network Layers
comments.explanation: The figure caption is too minimal to be useful. It shows four network layers with different sparsity levels but provides no explanation of how these sparse patterns were generated, what the P-Sparse concept means, or how readers should interpret the visual differences between 0.3, 0.5, and 0.7 sparsity. The caption should include a brief description of the P-Sparse methodology and clarify that these are illustrative examples of sparse connectivity patterns at initialization.

comments.title: Insufficient training specification
comments.quote: The autoencoder, two and three layered neural networks are trained in a standard manner with adam optimizer and batch update.
comments.explanation: This sentence is too vague for reproducibility. 'Standard manner' and 'batch update' don't specify critical hyperparameters needed to replicate the experiments. The paper should state the learning rate, batch size, number of epochs, and any weight decay used. Without these details, readers cannot verify whether the reported performance differences are due to the pruning methods or training configuration differences.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 missing fitness function definition in pseudocode
comments.quote: for every 5 iterations do pop individual with minimum sum (of weights) from KI;
comments.explanation: The algorithm description mentions using a fitness function but the pseudocode only references weight sums. According to the paper's description, fitness evaluation should determine both selection and elimination. The pseudocode should explicitly call the fitness function rather than using weight sum directly. Replace 'pop individual with minimum sum (of weights)' with 'evaluate fitness for all individuals; pop individual with minimum fitness' and similarly replace 'individual with maximum sum (of weights)' with 'individual with maximum fitness' to maintain consistency with the described methodology.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 missing termination condition for population depletion
comments.quote:
ections;
for maxiterations do Run Neural Network; Update weights; if One individual in KI is left then W $\leftarrow$ individual; else W $\leftarrow$ individual with maximum sum (of weights); for every 5 iterations do pop individual with minimum sum (of weights) from
comments.explanation: The algorithm eliminates one individual every 5 iterations but lacks a mechanism to replenish the population. With K initial individuals, after 5×(K-1) iterations, only one individual would remain, after which no further elimination occurs. This creates an unintended transition from evolutionary search to single-individual optimization. The algorithm should either specify a population replenishment strategy or clarify that this transition to single-individual optimization is intentional. Add 'if |KI| < K then add new random individual to KI' after the elimination step to maintain population size.

comments.title: Binary mask contradicts gradient accumulation purpose
comments.explanation: The algorithm sets accumulated_dW to either 0 or 1, creating a binary mask that eliminates all gradient magnitude information. This contradicts the stated purpose of "accumulating" gradients over epochs. If the goal is to track accumulated gradient magnitudes, accumulated_dW should preserve its values. The current implementation effectively implements a hard thresholding scheme, not gradient accumulation. Replace the binary assignment with "accumulated_dW ← accumulated_dW .* (abs(accumulated_dW) >= 0.0001);" to preserve gradient magnitudes while zeroing small values.

comments.title: Ambiguous optimization function specification
comments.quote: The network architectures are two layered Autoencoder (784-128-64), a three layered NN (784-128-100-10) both with sigmoid activation and adam optimization functions.
comments.explanation: The text states 'adam optimization functions' (plural) but Adam is a single optimization algorithm. This phrasing is ambiguous - does it mean Adam was used with different parameter settings for each architecture, or is this a grammatical error? The paper should clarify the exact Adam hyperparameters (learning rate, beta values, etc.) used across experiments, as these significantly impact training dynamics and comparability of results between architectures.

comments.title: Logical contradiction in dissipating gradients description
comments.quote: Dissipating gradients works well as long as the network isn't learning very fast i.e. some weights are being updated in the consequent epochs.
comments.explanation: This statement contains a logical contradiction. The condition 'network isn't learning very fast' directly conflicts with the explanation 'i.e. some weights are being updated in the consequent epochs.' If weights are being updated in subsequent epochs, this indicates the network IS learning. The authors should clarify whether the method works when (a) learning is slow overall but some weights still update, or (b) learning is minimal with few weight updates. Rewrite this sentence to resolve the contradiction: 'Dissipating gradients works well as long as the network's learning rate is moderate, ensuring that some weights continue to be updated in subsequent epochs while others remain stable.'

comments.title: False dichotomy in random dropout description
comments.quote: Either no useful weight is eliminated or multiple crucial weights are eliminated which decides how well does random dropout perform.
comments.explanation: This presents a false dichotomy by only considering two extreme outcomes. Random dropout can also eliminate some useful weights without eliminating multiple crucial ones, or eliminate a mix of useful and non-useful weights. The description should acknowledge the full spectrum of possibilities. Rewrite as: 'Random dropout's performance depends on which weights are eliminated - it may remove no useful weights, some useful weights, or multiple crucial weights, leading to variable performance.'

comments.title: Unsubstantiated reliability claim
comments.quote: It's also most reliable.
comments.explanation: The claim that dissipating gradients is 'most reliable' is presented without any supporting evidence or comparison to other methods. Reliability should be quantified through metrics like variance across runs, consistency of performance, or robustness to hyperparameter changes. Either provide empirical evidence showing lower variance or higher consistency compared to other methods, or remove this superlative claim.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated performance claim
comments.quote: The interesting result here is that isolated from all other factors like number of parameters, hidden units and various design choices, kstarts dropout performs better on a single layer network compared to even a fully connected network.
comments.explanation: This claim appears contradictory to the paper's own experimental setup. A single layer network with kstarts dropout is inherently a sparse version of the same network architecture, so it cannot be 'isolated from all other factors' when the key difference is the number of active parameters. The claim that a sparse network outperforms its dense counterpart on the same architecture needs concrete evidence from the figures, but the text doesn't reference specific data points or statistical significance. Without showing that the kstarts network achieves higher accuracy than the fully connected network at comparable computational cost, this claim remains unsupported. Rewrite this sentence to focus on the specific observed advantage (e.g., learning speed or stability) rather than making a general performance superiority claim.

comments.title: Section lacks substantive content
comments.quote: There are a number of design parameter choices for the algorithm 1 presented here. Some are explained in detail in this section.
comments.explanation: This section header promises to explain design parameter choices for Algorithm 1 in detail, but the section contains only this introductory sentence and no actual explanations. The section is essentially empty, which is a structural error. Either the detailed explanations should be provided here, or this section should be removed and its content integrated elsewhere. As written, this creates a false expectation for readers.

comments.title: Mathematical error in population definitions
comments.quote:
The second choice is to use gradient of the weights to create population as follows:

$$population = \delta W * K_{SparseMatrices} \quad (5)$$

doing so can have fitness totally dependant on the current update and the new weights are heavily skewed towards the performance of the current iterations which again doesn’t seem that appropriate.
- Sum of Gradients: The third option is summing up the gradients for a number of iterations in the manner we do for dissipating gradients 3 and then use those to create population:

$$population = (\sum \delta W) * K_{SparseMatrices} \quad (6)$$
comments.explanation: Equations 5 and 6 are mathematically incorrect for defining a population of weight matrices. If δW represents gradients, then multiplying gradients by sparse matrices creates gradient-based masks, not weight matrices. A population should consist of weight matrices W_i, not gradients. The correct formulation should be something like: W_i = W * K_i where K_i are sparse matrices, or W_i = W + α*(∑δW) * K_i if incorporating gradient information. As written, these equations don't produce valid weight matrix populations for the evolutionary algorithm.

comments.title: Inconsistent gradient notation
comments.quote:
The second choice is to use gradient of the weights to create population as follows:

$$population = \delta W * K_{SparseMatrices} \quad (5)$$
comments.explanation: The notation δW for gradients is inconsistent with standard mathematical conventions and potentially with other parts of the paper. Gradient notation should be ∂W/∂L or ∇W where L is the loss function. The use of δW typically denotes a small change or variation, not a gradient. This inconsistency creates ambiguity about whether the authors mean actual gradients, weight updates, or something else entirely.

comments.title: Grammatical error in possessive pronoun
comments.quote: we were able to concur that the method is effective in it’s own right.
comments.explanation: The text contains a grammatical error where 'it's' (contraction of 'it is') is used instead of 'its' (possessive pronoun). This should be corrected to maintain proper grammar. Rewrite 'we were able to concur that the method is effective in it's own right.' as 'we were able to concur that the method is effective in its own right.' because 'its' is the correct possessive form referring to the method.

comments.title: Terminology inconsistency in cost effectiveness section
comments.quote: One beneficial feature of pre-training pruning approaches is that the best p-sparse network is quickly identified. There are a number of methods that exploit sparsity of a matrix for quicker multiplication i.e. (Yuster & Zwick, 2005), (Buluç & Gilbert, 2012) which can be used to quickly retrain large networks. Although that is out of scope for our findings.
comments.explanation: The section title is 'Cost Effectiveness' but the content discusses computational speed benefits of sparse matrix multiplication rather than cost analysis. The text uses 'pre-training pruning approaches' which contradicts the paper's focus on 'pruning at initialization' as stated in the title and abstract. This terminology inconsistency creates confusion about whether the methods work before training begins or during pre-training phases. The section should either be retitled to reflect computational speed benefits or rewritten to address actual cost-effectiveness analysis like training time comparisons or computational overhead measurements.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated performance claims
comments.quote: We present two methods for pruning weights pre-training or in the first couple of epochs. The comparisons are made against random dropout and both approaches mostly perform better than the random dropout. We provide a combination dropout approach that consistently outperforms other dropout approaches.
comments.explanation: The formal results section only defines a population generation method using sparse matrices (equation 1), but provides no theorems, lemmas, or empirical results about performance comparisons with dropout methods. The discussion claims superior performance without any supporting formal analysis or proven results. The population definition alone cannot establish performance advantages over dropout approaches.

comments.title: Missing effectiveness justification
comments.quote: A lot more analysis of the approach is required on multiple datasets, learning tasks and network architectures but the basic methods seem to be effective.
comments.explanation: The formal results only present a mathematical definition of population generation without any analysis of effectiveness, convergence properties, or theoretical guarantees. The claim that methods 'seem to be effective' is not supported by any formal results in the paper, as no theorems or propositions about method effectiveness are provided.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes two initialization-based pruning strategies—K-starts and Dissipating Gradients—aiming to identify high-performing sparse subnetworks without training the full dense model. While the empirical results on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST show some promise, the methodology lacks rigorous comparison against established baselines and fails to address the computational overhead and scalability of the proposed search mechanisms.

The paper explores a relevant topic in sparse neural network training but suffers from a narrow experimental scope and a lack of comparative rigor. The absence of benchmarks against standard methods like magnitude pruning at initialization or iterative magnitude pruning makes it difficult to assess the actual contribution of the proposed heuristics. The results are currently too limited to support the broad claims regarding the efficacy of these pruning strategies.

Overview issues:

- Lack of Comparison Against Standard Baselines: The paper evaluates K-starts and Dissipating Gradients primarily against random dropout and fully connected networks. It fails to include comparisons with established pruning-at-initialization methods such as SNIP, GraSP, or SynFlow, which are standard in the field. Without benchmarking against these approaches, it is impossible to determine if the proposed methods offer any genuine advantage in terms of accuracy or computational efficiency. I recommend including these baselines to provide a meaningful context for the performance gains claimed.

- Inadequate Experimental Scope and Architecture Diversity: The experiments are restricted to simple architectures (single/three-layer MLPs and autoencoders) on small datasets (MNIST/Fashion-MNIST). This setup is insufficient for a paper claiming to address computational cost and scaling in modern neural networks. Modern pruning research requires validation on deeper architectures like ResNet or VGG and larger datasets like CIFAR-10 or ImageNet to demonstrate that the findings are not artifacts of simple, low-dimensional optimization tasks. The authors should extend their experiments to include at least one standard convolutional architecture.

- Unclear Computational Overhead Analysis: The paper argues that the proposed methods are efficient, yet the K-starts algorithm involves maintaining K sparse matrices and performing multiple forward passes to determine 'fitness'. The paper does not provide a formal analysis or empirical measurement of the total computational cost of the search process versus training a dense model. If the search process takes longer than standard training, the benefits of initialization pruning are negated. A clear breakdown of the time and memory complexity of the search phase is required to justify the practical utility of these methods.

- Methodological Flaws in Fitness Functions: The fitness function in Equation 2, which relies on the sum of weights or accumulated gradients, is not well-motivated. The authors acknowledge in Section 6.1 that magnitude-based selection might render the pruning process pointless by skewing the network toward a single sparse matrix early on. Furthermore, there is no discussion of how these criteria interact with batch normalization or weight initialization schemes (e.g., Xavier vs. Kaiming), which are known to influence gradient flow significantly at initialization. The paper needs a more rigorous justification for why these specific heuristics are expected to find 'winning tickets' rather than just pruning based on noise.

- Lack of empirical evidence for the 'Combination Dropout' performance claim: The paper claims that a combination of Kstarts and dissipating gradients 'consistently outperforms' other dropout approaches, yet this claim is not supported by a comparative table or plot that includes the individual methods versus the combined one. Readers cannot verify the magnitude of this improvement or determine if the gain is statistically significant. I suggest adding a performance comparison plot or table that explicitly shows the accuracy of Kstarts alone, dissipating gradients alone, and the combined approach across the same network architectures and datasets used in the study. This should be accompanied by a brief discussion on whether the combined approach introduces any additional hyperparameter tuning requirements compared to the individual methods.

- Absence of a concrete example demonstrating the 'winning ticket' property: The paper relies heavily on the intuition that its methods identify 'winning tickets' similar to the lottery ticket hypothesis, but it never demonstrates this property for the proposed sparse networks. To justify this claim, the authors should perform a 're-initialization' experiment: take the sparse mask identified by Kstarts or dissipating gradients, reset the weights to their original initialization values, and verify if the network still achieves the reported performance. Without this, it is unclear if the performance gains are due to the mask itself or simply the specific weight values found during the initial training phase.

- Missing justification for the choice of p-sparsity: The paper treats the connectivity factor 'p' as a static parameter but provides no guidance on how it should be selected for different architectures or tasks. Since the authors claim the method is effective, they should compute the performance of their models for a range of p values (e.g., 0.1 to 0.9) and present the results in a sensitivity analysis plot. This would clarify whether there is an 'optimal' sparsity level that generalizes across the tested MLPs and autoencoders, or if the method requires task-specific tuning that undermines the claim of being a general pre-training pruning approach.

Recommendation: reject

Revision targets:

- Include comparisons against state-of-the-art initialization pruning methods like SNIP, GraSP, and SynFlow.

- Expand experimental validation to include at least one convolutional architecture (e.g., ResNet-20) on a more complex dataset like CIFAR-10.

- Provide a formal analysis of the computational overhead of the K-starts search process relative to the total training time.

- Address the sensitivity of the pruning criteria to different weight initialization schemes and explain how the proposed methods avoid layer collapse.

comments.title: Mathematical ambiguity in population definition
comments.quote: population = W * K_{SparseMatrices} \quad
comments.explanation: The expression for the population is ill-defined. If W is a weight matrix of shape (m, n) and K_SparseMatrices is a collection of K masks, the operation is not standard matrix multiplication. If you intend an element-wise Hadamard product, the result is a set of K matrices, not a single 'population' object. Please clarify the dimensionality of K_SparseMatrices and the specific operation used to construct the population.

comments.title: Ambiguity in fitness function rationale
comments.quote: fittest = \arg \max_{ind} \sum_{j=1}^{i*c} ind[j] (\text{of population}) \quad (2)
comments.explanation: Using the sum of elements as a fitness metric is problematic for standard weight initializations like Xavier or Kaiming, which are zero-centered. This approach effectively prioritizes individuals based on the variance of initialization noise rather than structural utility. Please provide a justification for why this sum correlates with performance or define a fitness function that accounts for the expected value of the weights.

comments.title: Missing empirical evidence for combination dropout
comments.quote: Combination of dissipating gradients and kstarts outperforms either methods and random dropout consistently.
comments.explanation: The claim that the combination method consistently outperforms individual approaches is not supported by any data in the paper. Please include a performance comparison table or plot that explicitly shows the accuracy of K-starts alone, dissipating gradients alone, and the combined approach across the same architectures to substantiate this claim.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated convergence claim
comments.quote: For instance, if the iterations for the experiments in figure 3a and 3b are increased, the fully connected network will eventually reach accuracy of the p-sparse network.
comments.explanation: This assertion is presented without empirical support or theoretical justification. In many optimization settings, sparse networks may converge to different local optima than dense networks. Please provide extended training curves or a reference to an experiment that demonstrates this convergence behavior.

comments.title: Ambiguous training procedure
comments.quote: The autoencoder, two and three layered neural networks are trained in a standard manner with adam optimizer and batch update.
comments.explanation: The term 'batch update' is ambiguous. It is unclear if you mean stochastic gradient descent with a batch size of 1, mini-batch gradient descent, or full-batch gradient descent. Please specify the batch size used in your experiments, as this significantly impacts convergence.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes heuristic pruning methods but contains critical implementation flaws, specifically in the Dissipating Gradients algorithm, and conceptual errors regarding the training of sparse weights. The comparison baselines are also flawed, making the current results unreliable.

The paper proposes heuristic pruning methods but contains significant implementation flaws (specifically in the Dissipating Gradients algorithm) and conceptual errors regarding the training of sparse weights. The comparison baselines are also flawed, making the current results unreliable.

Overview issues:

- Implementation flaw in Dissipating Gradients algorithm: The pseudocode (Algorithm 2) sets the accumulated gradient sum to either 0 or 1 based on a threshold, then multiplies the weight matrix by this binary scalar. This prunes the entire layer if the sum of gradients is small, rather than pruning individual weights with low gradient magnitude as described in the text. This logic is mathematically inconsistent with the stated goal of eliminating weights that are not being updated.

- K-starts method does not train sparse weights: The paper claims to find 'trainable' sparse networks (Lottery Tickets). However, Algorithm 1 simply multiplies the weight matrix $W$ by a sparse matrix $KI$ and updates $W$ to be the mask itself. The weights *inside* the sparse structure are never updated via backpropagation; the network remains a dense network with masked-out connections. This fails to demonstrate that the sparse structure itself is the source of performance.

- Unfair comparison against standard Dropout: The paper compares its methods against 'Random Dropout.' However, standard Dropout applies a random mask *during* training to regularize the network. The paper's methods (and the 'random dropout' baseline implied) apply a static mask at initialization. Comparing a static mask to a dynamic regularization technique is methodologically flawed and obscures whether the improvement comes from the pruning criterion or the ability to train without regularization.

- Absence of CNN experiments: The paper only tests on simple MLPs and Autoencoders. CNNs are the dominant architecture for pruning research due to their high parameter count and structured sparsity. A paper on pruning at initialization without CNN results cannot be considered complete for a top-tier venue like ICLR.

- Lack of theoretical justification for heuristics: The 'K-starts' method uses the sum of weights as a fitness function. There is no theoretical justification for why the sum of a binary mask correlates with the ability of a subnetwork to learn. The method relies on a generic genetic algorithm framework and does not provide new insight into *why* pruning at initialization works.

Recommendation: Major Revision. The core ideas are fixable (rewrite the algorithm, add CNNs), but the current state contains critical implementation errors and weak baselines that prevent publication.

Revision targets:

- Correct the logic in Algorithm 2 to actually prune individual weights based on gradient magnitude, not layer-wise binary masking.

- Revise the K-starts method to actually train the weights within the sparse structure, rather than just masking a dense network.

- Add experiments on CNN architectures (e.g., LeNet or a small ResNet) to demonstrate the method's applicability to real-world models.

- Replace the 'random dropout' baseline with a proper stochastic dropout implementation to ensure a fair comparison.

comments.title: Population definition mismatch
comments.quote:
[approximate] The resulting set of matrices is our population for that iteration.

$$population = W * K_{SparseMatrices} \quad (1)$$
comments.explanation: The text describes the population as a 'set of matrices,' but Equation (1) defines `population` as a single matrix product. This notation is ambiguous. The equation should explicitly show that the population consists of the individual products, e.g., $Population = \{W \cdot S_i\}_{i=1}^K$.

comments.title: K-starts algorithmic contradiction
comments.quote:
[approximate] Every time the weight W needs to be updated instead of the usual gradient update we add one step further and using the fitness function, pass the fittest individual as the new W which biases the network towards that sparse matrix.

Run Neural Network; Update weights; if One individual in KI is left then W $\leftarrow$ individual; else W $\leftarrow$ individual with maximum sum (of weights);
comments.explanation: The pseudocode instructs the network to 'Update weights' via backpropagation, but then immediately overwrites the learned weights with a static sparse matrix. This prevents the network from actually training; it merely switches between sparse masks every iteration. The algorithm should either remove the weight update step from the main loop or separate the mask selection from the training phase.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes two pre-training pruning methods, K-starts and dissipating gradients, aimed at identifying sparse subnetworks early in training without retraining. The motivation is straightforward and the experimental setup is simple, but the execution contains several structural and methodological gaps that weaken the claims.

The work tackles a relevant problem in efficient deep learning by exploring initialization-based pruning. The authors correctly identify that early-stage gradient behavior and random sparse masks can influence network selection. However, the proposed fitness criterion, missing computational benchmarks, and limited baseline comparisons prevent the paper from establishing a clear or competitive contribution.

Overview issues:

- The fitness function in K-starts relies on weight magnitudes rather than task-relevant performance: Equation 2 defines the fitness of an individual sparse matrix as the sum of its weights, and Algorithm 1 selects the matrix with the maximum sum. This metric has no connection to learning ability or generalization, especially early in training when weights are randomly initialized. The authors acknowledge this weakness in Section 6.1, noting that magnitude-based selection skews the population and renders elimination pointless, yet they proceed to use it as the core selection mechanism. Because the method claims to find the fittest subnetwork, relying on an uncorrelated proxy compromises the evolutionary analogy and weakens result reproducibility. The authors should replace the magnitude-based fitness with a task-specific validation metric, such as loss on a held-out set, and demonstrate that the new criterion actually selects matrices that improve downstream performance.

- Claims of computational efficiency lack empirical support: The introduction and abstract repeatedly emphasize that initialization pruning reduces computational cost and scales efficiently, yet Section 8 explicitly admits that sparse matrix multiplication algorithms are not implemented and no time comparisons are provided. A pruning method that cannot demonstrate actual inference speedups or memory reduction fails to deliver on its primary motivation. Readers will naturally question whether the proposed masks actually translate to hardware acceleration or merely add masking overhead without benefit. The revision must include concrete computational benchmarks, such as inference latency and training throughput, using standard sparse tensor libraries to validate the claimed efficiency gains.

- Baseline comparisons omit established initialization pruning methods: The paper positions its work against random dropout and post-training pruning, but completely omits direct comparisons to initialization pruning baselines like Frankle et al. (2020) or magnitude-based early stopping. Random dropout is a regularization technique rather than a structural pruning method, making the comparison structurally mismatched. Furthermore, the claim that the proposed methods outperform random dropout is trivial and does not establish competitive standing in the pruning literature. To properly situate the contribution, the authors should benchmark against standard initialization pruning protocols, including random masking and gradient-based early stopping, across the same sparsity levels and training budgets. This will clarify whether the proposed methods offer genuine improvements over existing initialization strategies.

- Mathematical notation and algorithm description conflate masks with weight updates: Equation 1 writes population = W * K_SparseMatrices, which implies standard matrix multiplication, but the context clearly requires element-wise multiplication to apply sparsity masks. Algorithm 1 then states W <- individual with maximum sum, which would overwrite the entire weight matrix with a sparse binary mask and break gradient flow unless the authors implicitly mean element-wise masking. This notational ambiguity makes the method impossible to implement as written and obscures how backpropagation continues after mask selection. The revision should explicitly define the Hadamard product, clarify whether masks are applied multiplicatively to weights, and provide pseudocode that distinguishes between weight updates and mask selection.

- Critical hyperparameters lack theoretical grounding or ablation studies: The dissipating gradients method relies on pruning weights after only a few epochs, with the exact number of epochs determined by vague criteria like monochrome images or slow gradient dissipation in Section 2.2. Section 7.2 further suggests that a connectivity factor of 0.5 works best but admits this is wishful speculation requiring further analysis. The paper provides no systematic sensitivity analysis on the number of accumulation epochs, the epsilon threshold, or the impact of different learning rates on gradient dissipation. Without empirical validation across different network depths and data distributions, the method appears heavily tuned to MNIST-scale MLPs. The authors should include a detailed ablation study varying epoch counts, epsilon values, and dataset complexities to establish robust design guidelines.

- Worked example validating the gradient dissipation heuristic: The dissipating gradients heuristic needs validation because it assumes that weights with small accumulated gradients are unimportant. This claim lacks theoretical or empirical grounding in a controlled setting. Readers will wonder why a vanishing accumulated gradient reliably signals redundancy rather than convergence or a flat loss landscape. The authors should compute the accumulated gradient for a simple, fully-specified model like linear regression or a 2-layer network on a synthetic dataset. Showing analytically or numerically when the heuristic correctly prunes irrelevant weights versus when it incorrectly prunes important ones would calibrate intuition and validate the method's core assumption.

- Precise algorithmic definition of the combination pruning strategy: The combination pruning strategy lacks a precise algorithmic definition in the current draft. Section 2.3 describes merging kstarts and dissipating gradients but leaves the mechanics vague. It states the method fixes p for Kstarts and further eliminates weights, but does not specify whether the final mask is the intersection of both masks, a sequential application, or a weighted combination. Algorithm 1 and 2 only detail the individual methods. Without a clear pseudocode or mathematical formulation for the combination step, the method cannot be reproduced or fairly compared. The authors should add a dedicated algorithm block or equation defining exactly how the two sparsity masks are merged and applied to the weight matrix during training.

- Empirical analysis linking sparsity to generalization error: The claim that sparsity reduces generalization error remains underdeveloped despite repeated assertions in the abstract and introduction. The experiments only report mean accuracy and standard deviation on validation sets. The paper does not demonstrate the generalization gap, nor does it discuss how sparsity influences the bias-variance tradeoff in this context. To deliver on the stated implication, the authors should include learning curves comparing training and validation loss, or report test set performance on held-out data. Additionally, a brief discussion connecting the observed accuracy improvements to generalization bounds or empirical variance would strengthen the argument that sparsity actively prevents overfitting rather than merely matching performance.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Replace the magnitude-based fitness function in K-starts with a task-relevant metric (e.g., validation loss or gradient alignment) and report performance differences.

- Add computational benchmarks measuring inference latency, parameter reduction, and training throughput using sparse tensor operations to validate efficiency claims.

- Benchmark against standard initialization pruning methods (e.g., random masking, Frankle et al. 2020, magnitude pruning) rather than relying solely on random dropout.

- Clarify the mathematical notation by explicitly defining the Hadamard product and distinguishing between weight updates and mask application in Algorithm 1.

- Provide a systematic ablation study on the number of accumulation epochs, epsilon threshold, and connectivity factor p across multiple architectures and datasets.

comments.title: Misattribution of Hessian-based pruning to dropout
comments.quote: The post-training pruning has several approaches in place such as adding various regularization schemes to prune the network Louizos et al. (2017); Pan et al. (2016) or using second derivative or hessian of the weights for dropout LeCun et al. (1990); Hassibi and Stork
comments.explanation: The text incorrectly claims that LeCun et al. (1990) and Hassibi and Stork (1993) use second derivatives or Hessians 'for dropout.' These papers actually describe Optimal Brain Damage and Optimal Brain Surgeon, which are foundational methods for pruning weights based on second-order derivatives, not dropout. Dropout is a distinct regularization technique that randomly masks units, typically associated with Srivastava et al. (2014). Conflating these two lines of work misrepresents the literature and confuses the reader about what the cited methods actually do. Rewrite the sentence to separate Hessian-based weight pruning from dropout, and cite Frankle and Carbin (2018) or standard pruning surveys for the Hessian methods.

comments.title: Incorrect citation for the subnetwork learning hypothesis
comments.quote: We use a simple intuitive models that achieve good results and exploits the fact that a number of sub networks in a Neural Network has the potential to individually learn the input Srivastava et al. (2014).
comments.explanation: The authors attribute the existence of trainable subnetworks to Srivastava et al. (2014), which introduced dropout as a regularization technique. The explicit demonstration that randomly initialized subnetworks can learn effectively is the central finding of the lottery ticket hypothesis (Frankle and Carbin, 2018) and related work on overparameterization (Denil et al., 2013). Citing dropout for this property mischaracterizes the prior art and obscures the paper's actual motivation. The authors should replace the Srivastava citation with Frankle and Carbin (2018) or Denil et al. (2013) when discussing the learnability of subnetworks, and reserve Srivastava et al. (2014) for the dropout baseline discussion.

comments.title: Ambiguous mathematical definition of population
comments.quote: The basic components of the algorithm are: (1) Population: A product of network weights and sparse matrices. (2) Individual: An instance of the population. (3) Fitness Function: The heuristic chosen for evaluation of the population.
comments.explanation: Describing the population as 'a product' is mathematically imprecise and conceptually misleading. In evolutionary algorithms, a population is a set of candidate solutions, not a single multiplicative result. Additionally, 'product' is ambiguous here: it could mean standard matrix multiplication or the Hadamard (element-wise) product. Given that sparse matrices act as binary masks, element-wise multiplication is required. The paper's own Equation 1 uses a similar notation that the authors acknowledge implies standard multiplication but actually requires element-wise application. Rewrite the definition to state that the population consists of a set of candidate weight matrices generated by applying sparse masks to the initial weights, and explicitly specify that the operation is element-wise.

comments.title: Equation 1 misidentifies a single matrix instance and uses invalid set notation
comments.quote: We first initialize K sparse matrices, a single instance of these K sparse matrices can be seen in equation 1. In every iteration we multiply model weights W of the Network layer in question with every instance of the K sparse matrices. The resulting set of matrices is our population for that iteration.
comments.explanation: The text explicitly claims that Equation 1 shows 'a single instance' of the sparse matrices, but the equation actually defines the entire population. Additionally, the equation uses a single `*` operator to equate a `population` variable to the product of `W` and `K_{SparseMatrices}`, which is mathematically ill-defined for a collection of matrices. Since the population is a set of K matrices, the notation must reflect a set or indexed collection. Rewrite the sentence to clarify that Equation 1 defines the population generation step, and replace the equation with proper set notation such as $ ackslash mathcal{P}_t =  ackslash left ackslash { W_t  ackslash odot M_k  ackslash right ackslash }_{k=1}^K$ to accurately represent the resulting set of matrices.

comments.title: Population discard mechanism contradicts K-starts procedure
comments.quote: Every 5 generations or as per the decided elimination frequency, the individual with lowest fitness is discarded from the population.
comments.explanation: This bullet point describes a population-based evolutionary strategy, but K-starts does not maintain a population across cycles. The method generates a fixed set of K sparse matrices, ranks them by fitness, and selects the single best matrix for training. There is no ongoing population to prune, nor is there a mechanism for periodic elimination. The description borrows language from genetic algorithms without mapping it to the actual pruning pipeline. Remove this bullet point entirely or replace it with a precise account of how the K candidates are ranked and the winning mask is extracted.

comments.title: Contradiction between pruning rule and convergence behavior
comments.quote: Moreover, once specific weights have reached their optimal learning, their gradients will dissipate and we don’t want to eliminate them.
comments.explanation: This statement directly conflicts with the pruning criterion in Equation (4). When gradients dissipate near convergence, the accumulated value approaches zero, which satisfies the condition Accumulated_dw < epsilon and triggers W=0. The authors explicitly state they wish to preserve converged weights, yet the formula removes them. The method requires a mechanism to distinguish early stagnation from late-stage convergence, such as requiring small gradients over multiple consecutive epochs or tracking the magnitude of weight changes rather than just the gradient sum.

comments.title: Pseudocode loop structure and pruning frequency are syntactically invalid
comments.quote: for every 5 iterations do pop individual with minimum sum (of weights) from KI; end
comments.explanation: The algorithm block uses invalid pseudocode syntax and contains a logical flaw that prevents correct implementation. The phrase for every 5 iterations do is not a standard control structure and incorrectly nests the pruning operation inside the main training loop without a conditional guard. It should be rewritten as a conditional check, such as if iteration % 5 == 0 then. Additionally, the pop operation executes unconditionally every five steps, which will crash when KI contains only one individual. The length check if One individual in KI is left must either precede the pruning step or wrap it in an else block. Rewrite the loop body to explicitly check iteration % 5 == 0 and |KI| > 1 before removing the worst individual.

comments.title: Undefined scalar comparison to gradient tensor
comments.quote: if accumulated_dW < 0.0001 then
comments.explanation: The pseudocode compares the accumulated gradient tensor directly to a scalar, which is mathematically undefined. Additionally, the inequality < 0.0001 will trigger for large negative accumulated gradients, incorrectly pruning weights that are actually changing significantly. The condition should use an absolute value or norm, such as if |accumulated_dW| < 0.0001 then, to correctly identify near-zero gradients.

comments.title: Missing reset of gradient accumulator across epochs
comments.explanation: The algorithm never resets accumulated_dW to zero at the start of each epoch or after the threshold check. Without resetting, the accumulator sums gradients over all epochs, contradicting the stated goal of using only the first 1-4 epochs. The authors should insert accumulated_dW <- 0; at the beginning of the outer loop or immediately after the pruning step to ensure epoch-wise accumulation.

comments.title: Contradiction between figure title and methodology description
comments.quote: Figure 3: Effect of sample size on learning when varying the sparsity of the network with kstarts. Each trial involves running a p-sparse network on a fixed training set of N examples from dataset for 2.5k iterations and is averaged over 10 trials each.
comments.explanation: The figure title claims to show the effect of sample size, yet the caption explicitly states that each trial uses a 'fixed training set of N examples.' If the training set size is fixed, the experiment cannot demonstrate how varying sample size impacts learning. This logical mismatch makes the figure's purpose unclear. The authors should verify whether N actually varies across the subplots or experimental conditions. If N is constant, the title must be changed to reflect the actual variable being tested. If N does vary, the caption must be rewritten to describe the range of N values used.

comments.title: Fitness function description contradicts algorithm definition
comments.quote:
given  $k$  is large enough and the fitness function is smartly decided by first examining the weights and gradients.

Diss
comments.explanation: The authors state that the fitness function examines both weights and gradients. This directly contradicts the method's actual implementation, where Equation 2 defines fitness strictly as the sum of weight magnitudes. The introduction of gradients into the fitness calculation here is inconsistent with the paper's core design choice to avoid task-specific metrics. Readers will be confused about whether the method actually uses gradients for selection or if this is a typographical slip. Rewrite the clause as 'given k is large enough and the fitness function is determined solely by examining the weights' because the algorithm explicitly uses only weight magnitudes for selection.

comments.title: Logical contradiction in dissipating gradients condition
comments.quote: Dissipating gradients works well as long as the network isn't learning very fast i.e. some weights are being updated in the consequent epochs.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'isn't learning very fast i.e. some weights are being updated' contains a logical contradiction that obscures the intended claim. If a network is not learning quickly, gradient magnitudes tend to vanish uniformly, which would make gradient-based pruning unreliable rather than effective. The authors almost certainly meant that the method works well when the network is learning fast, allowing the algorithm to distinguish between important weights (large updates) and redundant ones (small updates). Correct the statement to reflect that active learning and substantial weight updates are prerequisites for the heuristic to function correctly.

comments.title: Contradictory architectural comparison and terminology
comments.quote: The interesting result here is that isolated from all other factors like number of parameters, hidden units and various design choices, kstarts dropout performs better on a single layer network compared to even a fully connected network.
comments.explanation: The sentence presents a direct logical contradiction. The authors claim the comparison isolates factors like 'hidden units,' yet they compare a 'single layer network' to a 'fully connected network.' A single-layer network with dense connections is by definition fully connected. If the authors mean to compare a single-layer network against a multi-layer network, they are explicitly varying the number of hidden units, which violates their own claim of isolation. If they mean to compare two fully connected networks of identical size, the terminology is incorrect. The authors should specify the exact network depths and parameter counts in Figures 3a and 3b, and revise the claim to accurately reflect which architectural factors were controlled.

comments.title: Conflation of population and fitness in gradient alternatives
comments.quote:
cond choice is to use gradient of the weights to create population as follows:

$$population = \delta W * K_{SparseMatrices} \quad (5)$$
comments.explanation: The section explicitly frames these three bullets as alternatives for the fitness function, yet equations (5) and (6) redefine the population variable. In the first bullet, the authors correctly distinguish between the population (a set of candidate matrices) and the fitness (a scalar metric used for selection). Equations (5) and (6) output a matrix of the same dimensions as the weights, which cannot function as a scalar fitness score for ranking individuals. If the intent is to propose a gradient-based fitness metric, the equations should compute a scalar value, such as the norm or sum of the masked gradients. If the intent is to propose a gradient-based mask, the left-hand side should not be labeled population. Rewriting the equations to explicitly define a scalar fitness function or renaming the variable to reflect a mask would resolve this ambiguity.

comments.title: Ambiguous terminology 'batch update' lacks standard definition
comments.quote: The autoencoder, two and three layered neural networks are trained in a standard manner with adam optimizer and batch update.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'batch update' is non-standard and ambiguous in the context of neural network optimization. It is unclear whether the authors mean full-batch gradient descent, mini-batch stochastic gradient descent, or online updates. Since the choice of batch size directly impacts optimization dynamics and convergence behavior, this term should be replaced with precise terminology (e.g., 'mini-batch gradient descent with batch size X') or the batch size should be explicitly specified. Without clarification, readers cannot accurately reproduce the training procedure or compare the optimization setup to other initialization pruning methods.

comments.title: Inconsistent enumeration of proposed methods
comments.quote: In approbation of the above reasons, the paper presents two approaches to prune at initialization. The goal is to achieve higher sparsity while preserving performance. 1) K-starts, begins with k random p-sparse matrices
comments.explanation: The abstract explicitly states the paper presents 'two approaches,' yet immediately follows with a numbered list of three items. The third item describes a combination strategy, which is presented as a distinct approach rather than a derivative of the first two. This creates a direct contradiction in the scope of the paper. Readers will be confused about whether the combination method is a core contribution or an afterthought. Rewrite 'presents two approaches' to 'presents three approaches' or restructure the list to present the combination as a synthesis of the first two methods rather than a third independent approach.

comments.title: Ambiguous terminology for network depth and layers
comments.quote: We initially use single layer NN (784-10) to isolate the effects of kstarts algorithm from effects of other parameters that may have a large impact on performance as the size of the network grows.
comments.explanation: The description of the architectures relies on non-standard and ambiguous phrasing. Single layer NN (784-10) typically denotes a linear model or single-layer perceptron with no hidden layers, yet the text later contrasts this with a three layered NN, which could mean three total layers or three hidden layers. This inconsistency makes it difficult to compare the experimental setups. Furthermore, referring to a linear mapping as a neural network without mentioning activation functions may mislead readers about the model's capacity. Clarify the exact dimensions and layer counts for every architecture tested, and specify whether layered refers to total layers or hidden layers.

comments.title: Misattributed citation for sparse matrix multiplication
comments.quote: There are a number of methods that exploit sparsity of a matrix for quicker multiplication i.e. (Yuster & Zwick, 2005), (Buluç & Gilbert, 2012) which can be used to quickly retrain large networks.
comments.explanation: The reference to Yuster & Zwick (2005) is misplaced in this context. That paper presents algorithms for fast dense rectangular matrix multiplication, not sparse matrix multiplication. Citing it alongside Buluç & Gilbert (2012), which specifically addresses sparse routines, creates a factual inaccuracy. Furthermore, sparse multiplication algorithms primarily accelerate matrix-vector products during forward and backward passes; they do not inherently speed up the full training loop, which includes optimizer steps and gradient accumulation. Rewrite the passage to remove the Yuster & Zwick (2005) citation and clarify that sparse multiplication accelerates matrix-vector products rather than full training loops, because the cited paper covers dense matrix multiplication and the current claim overstates the algorithm's scope.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes two pruning-at-initialization methods (KStarts and Dissipating Gradients) and their combination. The core ideas are plausible but the paper suffers from severe under-specification of methodology, lack of proper baselines, and an experimental evaluation that is far too narrow to support the claims made. The writing is often unclear and the algorithms are described at a level that makes reproduction difficult.

The paper tackles an interesting problem—pruning at initialization—and proposes two conceptually distinct approaches. However, the contribution is severely undermined by vague algorithmic descriptions, a lack of comparison to standard baselines (SNIP, GraSP, SynFlow), and experiments limited to tiny datasets and simple architectures. The paper does not meet the standards expected at a top venue like ICLR.

Overview issues:

- Missing comparison to standard pruning-at-initialization baselines: The paper compares only against 'random dropout' and does not include any of the established pruning-at-initialization methods: SNIP (Lee et al., 2019), GraSP (Wang et al., 2020), or SynFlow (Tanaka et al., 2020). Without these comparisons, it is impossible to assess whether the proposed methods offer any advantage over existing techniques. The paper claims to outperform random dropout, but that is a very low bar—many methods already do. The authors must include experiments with at least these three baselines under identical conditions (same architectures, datasets, sparsity levels) to substantiate their claims of superiority.

- Unclear algorithmic specification of KStarts: The KStarts algorithm is described at a very high level. It is unclear how the K sparse matrices are generated (what distribution? Are they fixed or resampled?), how the 'fitness function' is computed in practice (Equation 2 sums the weights of the masked network—but over what?), and how the weight update step interacts with the population selection. The pseudocode in Algorithm 1 is ambiguous: it says 'Update weights' but does not specify whether the standard gradient update is applied before or after the population selection. This makes the method irreproducible. The authors need to provide a precise, step-by-step description of the algorithm, including all mathematical operations and the order of operations.

- Dissipating Gradients algorithm is poorly defined and likely incorrect as written: Algorithm 2 contains a critical ambiguity: it sets `accumulated_dW` to 0 or 1 based on a threshold, then multiplies `W` by this binary mask. But `accumulated_dW` is a tensor of per-weight gradient sums, so the comparison `accumulated_dW < 0.0001` is element-wise, and the resulting mask is binary. However, the algorithm also says 'accumulated_dW ← accumulated_dW + dW' inside the iteration loop, which would accumulate gradients over iterations within an epoch, but the resetting logic is not specified. Moreover, the method eliminates weights whose accumulated gradient is below epsilon, but this is applied after only 1-4 epochs—far too early for gradients to stabilize. The authors must clarify the exact accumulation schedule, the thresholding rule, and provide a justification for why this heuristic should identify weights that are unimportant for the final task.

- Experimental evaluation is far too limited: The experiments are conducted only on MNIST and Fashion MNIST, using only fully-connected networks (single-layer, three-layer, and a small autoencoder). This is insufficient to demonstrate generalizability. Modern pruning-at-initialization papers routinely evaluate on CIFAR-10/100 with VGG and ResNet architectures, and ideally on ImageNet subsets. The paper's claims about 'superior performance' and 'effectiveness' are not supported by such narrow experiments. The authors should at minimum include experiments on CIFAR-10 with a convolutional architecture (e.g., VGG-16 or ResNet-20) and report accuracy vs. sparsity curves.

- No analysis of computational cost or practical utility: The paper does not report the computational overhead of the proposed methods. KStarts requires maintaining K copies of the weight matrix and evaluating a fitness function each iteration—this could be very expensive. Dissipating Gradients requires storing gradient sums over an epoch. The paper mentions that sparse matrix multiplication could be used but does not implement it. Without any timing or FLOPs comparison against baselines, it is unclear whether these methods offer any practical advantage. The authors should report the number of forward/backward passes, training time, and inference time for their methods versus baselines at comparable sparsity levels.

- The paper conflates pruning with dropout and misuses terminology: The paper repeatedly uses 'dropout' to refer to structured pruning (removing weights permanently), which is a different operation from the standard dropout that randomly zeros activations during training. This conflates two distinct techniques and will confuse readers. The paper should consistently use 'pruning' for the removal of weights and reserve 'dropout' for the stochastic regularization technique. Additionally, the term 'p-sparsity' is defined as the fraction of weights removed, but the paper later uses p=0.0 for fully connected and p=0.5 for 50% sparsity—this is the standard definition but the notation is non-standard and could be clarified.

- No theoretical justification or insight into why the methods work: The paper is purely empirical and provides no theoretical analysis of why the proposed pruning criteria should identify good subnetworks. For KStarts, the fitness function (sum of weights) is essentially magnitude-based pruning at initialization, which is known to be ineffective (Frankle et al., 2021). For Dissipating Gradients, the connection to gradient flow preservation is not made, and it is unclear why weights with small accumulated gradients after 1-4 epochs should be pruned. The paper would benefit from a simple analysis (e.g., showing that the masks correlate with final weight magnitudes or that they preserve gradient flow) to provide insight into the method's behavior.

Recommendation: reject. The paper does not meet the standards of a top venue: the methods are poorly specified, the experimental evaluation is far too narrow, and there is no comparison to standard baselines. Without major revisions including proper baselines, broader experiments, and clearer algorithmic descriptions, the paper is not publishable at ICLR.

Revision targets:

- Add experiments on CIFAR-10 with VGG and ResNet architectures, comparing against SNIP, GraSP, and SynFlow at multiple sparsity levels.

- Provide a precise, step-by-step algorithmic description of both KStarts and Dissipating Gradients, including all mathematical operations and order of operations, so that the methods are reproducible.

- Report computational cost (number of forward/backward passes, training time) for all methods to assess practical utility.

- Include a theoretical or empirical analysis that provides insight into why the proposed pruning criteria identify good subnetworks (e.g., correlation with final weight magnitudes, gradient flow preservation).

- Clarify terminology throughout: use 'pruning' instead of 'dropout' for weight removal, and define p-sparsity consistently with standard notation.

comments.title: Algorithm 2 has a critical logical error in the masking step
comments.explanation: The conditional `if accumulated_dW < 0.0001` applies a single scalar comparison to the entire tensor, which would either zero out all weights or keep all weights, rather than performing per-weight pruning. The intended behavior is element-wise thresholding. The fix is to replace the conditional block with an element-wise mask: `mask = (accumulated_dW >= 0.0001).float(); W = W * mask;` and not reassign `accumulated_dW` to a scalar. This is a genuine error in the pseudocode.

comments.title: Section 4 contains no algorithm details
comments.quote: The autoencoder, two and three layered neural networks are trained in a standard manner with adam optimizer and batch update.
comments.explanation: The section titled '4 ALGORITHM' contains only one sentence describing the training setup. It does not provide any algorithmic pseudocode, step-by-step procedure, or formal description of the proposed methods (KStarts or Dissipating Gradients). This is a critical omission because the paper claims to introduce new pruning algorithms, yet the section that should specify them is essentially empty. The authors must include full pseudocode for both KStarts and Dissipating Gradients, with clear mathematical notation for all operations, the order of operations, and the interaction between pruning mask updates and gradient updates. Without this, the methods are irreproducible.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 pseudocode is ambiguous and incomplete
comments.quote:
Algorithm 1: k Random starts
```txt
input: Data, params,K,p
output: W,b
initialize W,b;
$KI\gets K$ individuals with approximately 1-p percent active connections;
for maxiterations do Run Neural Network; Update weights; if One individual in KI is left then W $\leftarrow$ individual; else W $\leftarrow$ individual with maximum sum (of weights); for every 5 iterations do pop individual with minimum sum (of weights) from KI; end
end
```
comments.explanation: The pseudocode is critically underspecified. The line 'Update weights' does not indicate whether the standard gradient update is applied to the current W (which is a single weight matrix) or to each individual in KI separately. The condition 'if One individual in KI is left' is unclear because KI is initialized with K individuals and individuals are only removed every 5 iterations, so the condition would only be true after at least 5*(K-1) iterations, but the algorithm does not specify when the loop terminates. Moreover, the assignment 'W ← individual with maximum sum (of weights)' is ambiguous: does 'individual' refer to a sparse matrix from KI? If so, the assignment replaces the entire weight matrix W with that sparse matrix, but the algorithm does not specify how the bias b is handled. The algorithm also lacks a termination condition for the outer loop ('maxiterations' is not defined). To be reproducible, the authors must provide a precise, step-by-step description with all operations and their order, including how the gradient update interacts with the population selection. Rewrite the pseudocode with explicit loop bounds, clear assignment semantics, and a defined termination condition.

comments.title: Abstract claims retraining is unnecessary but contradicts later sections
comments.quote: Retraining of the model is neither necessary nor affects the performance of the p-sparse network.
comments.explanation: This claim is contradicted by the paper's own experimental setup. In Section 5, all experiments involve training the pruned network for multiple epochs after pruning—this is retraining. The statement that retraining 'does not affect performance' is also unsupported, as no experiment compares pruned networks with and without retraining. The authors should either remove this claim or provide evidence that the pruned network's performance is unchanged after additional training.

comments.title: Abstract claims about sparsity and epochs are internally inconsistent
comments.quote: Removing weights in this manner despite their magnitude best preserves performance of the network. Contrarily, the approach also takes the most epochs to achieve higher sparsity.
comments.explanation: The abstract states that Dissipating Gradients 'takes the most epochs to achieve higher sparsity,' yet earlier it says the method prunes after 'the first couple of epochs.' This is contradictory: if pruning happens after 1-2 epochs, the method cannot take 'the most epochs' unless other methods prune even earlier (e.g., at initialization). The authors should clarify the pruning schedule and reconcile these statements.

comments.title: Equation 1 dimension mismatch
comments.quote:
We first initialize K sparse matrices, a single instance of these K sparse matrices can be seen in equation 1. In every iteration we multiply model weights W of the Network layer in question with every instance of the K sparse matrices. The resulting set of matrices is our population for that iteration. Each iteration is referred to as a new generation.

$$population = W * K_{SparseMatrices} \quad (1)$$
comments.explanation: Equation 1 writes `population = W * K_{SparseMatrices}`. If `W` is a weight matrix (e.g., of shape `(m, n)`) and `K_{SparseMatrices}` is a set of `K` sparse matrices, the multiplication `W * K_{SparseMatrices}` is not a standard matrix operation. The paper does not specify whether `*` denotes element-wise multiplication, matrix multiplication, or a broadcast operation. If `K_{SparseMatrices}` is meant to be a 3D tensor of shape `(K, m, n)` and `W` is `(m, n)`, then `W * K_{SparseMatrices}` would be element-wise multiplication with broadcasting, producing `K` masked weight matrices. However, the text says 'multiply model weights W ... with every instance of the K sparse matrices,' which suggests a loop or tensor operation that is not clearly defined. The notation `K_{SparseMatrices}` is also ambiguous—it appears to denote both the number `K` and the set of matrices. Rewrite Equation 1 as `population = {W ⊙ M_i : i = 1, ..., K}` where `M_i` are the sparse mask matrices, to clarify the operation.

comments.title: Fitness function definition is ambiguous and incomplete
comments.quote:
The fitness of an individual is ranked by determining the sum of each individual in population as given in 1 such that the fittest individual in a generation is given by equation 2.

$$fittest = \arg \max_{ind} \sum_{j=1}^{i*c} ind[j] (\text{of population}) \quad (2)$$

where $i*c$ is the size of each individual and ind refers to the individual in population.
comments.explanation: The fitness function in Equation (2) is defined as the sum of the entries of the individual weight vector (or matrix). However, this is essentially magnitude-based pruning at initialization, which is known to be ineffective (Frankle et al., 2021). The paper does not justify why this simple sum should be a good proxy for subnetwork quality. Moreover, the equation references 'as given in 1' but no Equation (1) is provided in this section, making the definition incomplete. The authors should either provide Equation (1) or remove the reference. Additionally, the notation 'ind[j] (of population)' is ambiguous—it is unclear whether ind[j] refers to a weight value, a binary mask entry, or something else. The authors should clarify the exact meaning of ind[j] and provide a precise mathematical definition of the fitness function.

comments.title: Missing algorithmic detail for KStarts selection
comments.quote:
Assuming each iteration is the next generation. In each generation the fit individual is favoured so:

- The fittest individual is passed of as weight to the next generation.
- Every 5 generations or as per the decided elimination frequency, the individual with lowest fitness is discarded from the population.
comments.explanation: This description is too vague to be reproducible. It does not specify how the 'fittest individual' is determined—whether by the fitness function in Equation 2 (sum of weights) or by validation accuracy after a few epochs. The phrase 'passed of as weight' is ambiguous: does it mean the entire weight matrix of the fittest individual replaces that of the next generation, or is it used as a parent for crossover/mutation? Similarly, 'discarded from the population' does not clarify whether the individual is simply removed (reducing population size) or replaced by a new random individual. The elimination frequency 'every 5 generations or as per the decided elimination frequency' is underspecified—what determines this frequency? Without precise definitions, the algorithm cannot be implemented or compared. The authors should provide a complete pseudocode specifying the population initialization, fitness evaluation, selection, crossover/mutation (if any), and replacement strategy.

comments.title: Combination dropout description lacks specificity
comments.quote: Combination dropout is merely combining Kstarts with dissipating gradients. The weights eliminated use both approaches. We fix p for Kstarts to a certain value of minimum sparsity and further eliminate weights that dissipating gradients method will eliminate as well. The approach achieves better performance than either methods.
comments.explanation: The description of combination dropout is too vague to be reproducible. It does not specify how the two pruning methods are combined: are the masks intersected (weights pruned by either method are removed) or unioned (only weights pruned by both are removed)? The phrase 'further eliminate weights that dissipating gradients method will eliminate as well' suggests a sequential process where KStarts sets a base sparsity and then dissipating gradients removes additional weights, but the order and interaction are unclear. The authors should provide a precise algorithmic description, including how the two masks are merged and what happens if the total sparsity exceeds the target. Without this, the claim of 'better performance' cannot be verified or attributed to the combination strategy.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of p-sparsity
comments.quote: In a p-sparse network layer approximately p percent of connection between two layers are eliminated.
comments.explanation: The definition of p-sparsity is ambiguous. The phrase 'p percent of connection between two layers are eliminated' could mean that p% of connections are removed (so p=0.5 means 50% sparsity) or that p% remain (so p=0.5 means 50% density). The paper later uses p=0.0 for fully connected and p=0.5 for 50% sparsity, which suggests p is the fraction removed. However, the term 'p-sparse' is non-standard; most literature uses 'sparsity' to denote the fraction of zero weights. To avoid confusion, rewrite the definition as: 'In a layer with sparsity level p, approximately p × 100% of the connections between two layers are eliminated (i.e., set to zero).'

comments.title: Figure caption lacks explanation of P-sparsity
comments.quote: Figure 1: Sample P-Sparse Network Layers
comments.explanation: The figure caption is too brief. It does not explain what P-sparsity is, how the sparsity levels (0.0, 0.5, 0.3, 0.7) are defined, or what the visual elements (e.g., lines, nodes) represent. Without this context, readers cannot interpret the figure. Add a caption that defines P-sparsity as the fraction of weights removed and describes the network structure shown.

comments.title: Algorithm 2 does not specify how sparsity level is achieved
comments.quote: Algorithm 2 is more simple and just eliminates weights with sum of gradients equal to zero in the first 1-4 epochs depending on the desired sparsity.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that the algorithm eliminates weights with sum of gradients equal to zero 'depending on the desired sparsity,' but the pseudocode uses a fixed threshold of 0.0001 with no mechanism to achieve a specific sparsity level. The threshold is not tied to any sparsity target, so the algorithm cannot control the fraction of weights pruned. The authors should either parameterize the threshold based on a desired sparsity (e.g., prune the bottom k% of accumulated gradient magnitudes) or explain how the fixed threshold yields different sparsities across epochs.

comments.title: Inconsistent architecture description for three-layer NN
comments.quote: The network architectures are two layered Autoencoder (784-128-64), a three layered NN (784-128-100-10) both with sigmoid activation and adam optimization functions.
comments.explanation: The three-layer NN is described as (784-128-100-10), which lists four numbers. If this is a three-layer network, the numbers should represent input, two hidden layers, and output, but the notation (784-128-100-10) implies three hidden layers (128, 100) plus input and output, totaling four weight layers. The authors should clarify whether this is a three-layer or four-layer network, or adjust the notation to match the number of layers (e.g., (784-128-10) for a single hidden layer).

comments.title: Vague and non-quantitative description of results
comments.quote:
As sparsity increases, overall performance reduces. Figure 2 shows the behaviour of various dropout methods presented in this paper.

In case of random dropout, it's indeed a random shot. Either no useful weight is eliminated or multiple crucial weights are eliminated which decides how well does random dropout perform. Kstarts performs slightly better on average with multiple start choices. Depending on how many independent p-sparse networks are present in the network that can learn well, one of them can be identified, given  $k$  is large enough and the fitness function is smartly decided by first examining the weights and gradients.

Dissipating gradients works well as long as the network isn't learning very fast i.e. some weights are being updated in the consequent epochs. It's also most reliable. Combination works by far the best because it does not only rely on eliminating weights that are not being updated but also uses kstarts. It seems to achieve superior performance as long as p value chosen is a value that kstarts performs well on.
comments.explanation: This entire paragraph is a qualitative summary that does not cite any numerical results from Figure 2. The paper claims that 'Kstarts performs slightly better on average' and 'Dissipating gradients works well' and 'Combination works by far the best,' but it provides no accuracy numbers, no sparsity levels, no error bars, and no statistical comparison. Without concrete data (e.g., 'at 90% sparsity, KStarts achieves 97.2% accuracy vs. 96.5% for random dropout'), the claims are unverifiable and the section lacks scientific rigor. The authors should rewrite this section to report the actual accuracy values and standard deviations from Figure 2 for each method at multiple sparsity levels, and state which differences are statistically significant.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about Dissipating Gradients reliability
comments.quote: Dissipating gradients works well as long as the network isn't learning very fast i.e. some weights are being updated in the consequent epochs. It's also most reliable.
