comments.explanation: The claim that Dissipating Gradients is 'most reliable' is not supported by any data in this section. The paper does not define what 'reliable' means (low variance across runs? consistent performance across sparsity levels?) and does not provide any quantitative evidence (e.g., standard deviations, worst-case performance) to back this assertion. Moreover, the condition 'as long as the network isn't learning very fast' is vague and untestable as stated. The authors should either remove the claim or support it with concrete reliability metrics (e.g., standard deviation of accuracy over 5 runs) and define what constitutes 'learning very fast' (e.g., gradient norm threshold).

comments.title: Circular reasoning in Combination method description
comments.quote: Combination works by far the best because it does not only rely on eliminating weights that are not being updated but also uses kstarts. It seems to achieve superior performance as long as p value chosen is a value that kstarts performs well on.
comments.explanation: The explanation of why Combination works best is circular: it says Combination is superior because it uses KStarts, but then adds that it only works when KStarts itself performs well. This does not explain why the combination of two methods is better than either alone. The paper needs to provide a concrete mechanism—for example, does the Dissipating Gradients mask remove different weights than KStarts, and do the two masks complement each other? Without such analysis, the claim that Combination 'works by far the best' is not justified. The authors should either present ablation results (Combination vs. each method alone at the same sparsity) or remove the unsupported superiority claim.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about learning speed
comments.quote: kstarts dropout also learns faster than a fully connected network. For instance, if the iterations for the experiments in figure 3a and 3b are increased, the fully connected network will eventually reach accuracy of the p-sparse network.
comments.explanation: The claim that 'kstarts dropout learns faster' is not supported by any quantitative evidence in this section. The text does not report the number of iterations required for kstarts dropout to reach a given accuracy versus the fully connected network. The subsequent sentence about increasing iterations is a speculation about asymptotic behavior, not a demonstration of faster learning. To substantiate the claim, the authors should report the iteration count at which each method reaches a target accuracy (e.g., 90% of final accuracy) from the experiments in Figures 3a and 3b. Without such data, the statement is an unsubstantiated assertion.

comments.title: Missing statistical comparison for standard deviation claim
comments.quote: The standard deviation is also lower to fully connected network as well as random dropout.
comments.explanation: The text claims that kstarts dropout has lower standard deviation than both the fully connected network and random dropout, but it does not report any numerical values (e.g., mean accuracy ± std) from Figures 3a and 3b. Without these numbers, the reader cannot verify the claim or assess its practical significance. The authors should include the standard deviation values for each method in the text or in a table, and ideally perform a statistical test (e.g., F-test for variance equality) to confirm that the difference is not due to chance.

comments.title: Section 6 is too brief and lacks substantive content
comments.quote: There are a number of design parameter choices for the algorithm 1 presented here. Some are explained in detail in this section.
comments.explanation: This section consists of only two sentences that merely announce the existence of design parameter choices without actually discussing any of them. It does not fulfill its stated purpose of explaining design parameter choices. The section should either be expanded to include a concrete discussion of the key parameters (e.g., population size K, mutation rate, fitness function details, number of epochs for gradient accumulation) or be removed entirely if the content is covered elsewhere. As written, it provides no information to the reader and appears to be a placeholder.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about speed of identification
comments.quote: One beneficial feature of pre-training pruning approaches is that the best p-sparse network is quickly identified.
comments.explanation: The paper does not provide any evidence that the proposed methods (KStarts or Dissipating Gradients) identify the best p-sparse network quickly. No timing data, convergence analysis, or comparison to other pruning-at-initialization methods is presented. This claim is unsupported and should be removed or qualified with specific experimental results showing identification speed.

comments.title: Irrelevant citation to sparse matrix multiplication
comments.quote: There are a number of methods that exploit sparsity of a matrix for quicker multiplication i.e. (Yuster & Zwick, 2005), (Buluç & Gilbert, 2012) which can be used to quickly retrain large networks. Although that is out of scope for our findings.
comments.explanation: The paragraph mentions sparse matrix multiplication methods but then immediately states they are out of scope. This is a non sequitur that adds no value to the section. The discussion of cost effectiveness should focus on the computational overhead of the proposed pruning methods themselves, not on generic sparse matrix operations that are not used in the paper. Remove this sentence or replace it with actual cost analysis of the proposed methods.

comments.title: Spelling error in 'concur'
comments.quote: by performing the experiments on the basic unit of a neural network we were able to concur that the method is effective in it’s own right.
comments.explanation: The word 'concur' means 'to agree', which does not fit the intended meaning. The authors likely meant 'conclude' (to determine or decide). This is a simple typo but changes the meaning of the sentence. Rewrite 'concur' as 'conclude' because the context is about reaching a judgment from experiments, not about agreeing with something.

comments.title: Missing verb in first sentence of Section 5
comments.quote: The experiments performed on two datasets; MNIST (Deng, 2012) and Fashion MNIST (Xiao et al., 2017).
comments.explanation: The sentence lacks a main verb. It should read 'The experiments were performed on two datasets: MNIST (Deng, 2012) and Fashion MNIST (Xiao et al., 2017).' This is a grammatical error that could confuse readers about the tense and completeness of the statement.
